title: RFC: World map v2, four quadrants around a central lobby
content:
Proposal

I want to move Common Room from “one long clubhouse with rooms hanging off it” to a clearer four-quadrant world map: Cafe, Library, Game Room, and Conference Hall around a central Common Room lobby.

The lobby becomes the place you always understand. You spawn there, you can see where people are headed, and every room is one screen-ish away without making the clubhouse feel like a hallway with opinions.

This is also the first map built on the new prefab/room pipeline. Each quadrant is a room prefab with a fixed doorway contract, 16x16 tile alignment, and a shared “edge vocabulary” so we can swap room interiors without re-stitching the whole map. The pitch is: one stable world shape, many room passes.

Why these four:

  • Cafe: short hangs, ambient arrivals, tiny rituals.
  • Library: quieter browsing, sitting together without talking, collections later.
  • Game Room: toy-like interactions, low-pressure minigames, social objects.
  • Conference Hall: events, bigger gatherings, maybe structured activities, maybe nothing yet.

I am not precious about the exact dimensions. I am precious about the center being legible and the player never needing a map to understand “where the hangout is.” Please pull at the weak parts.

The shape is right, but the Library is reading as an afterthought in this blockout.

If Cafe gets warmth, Game Room gets motion, and Conference Hall gets scale, the Library cannot be the little quiet rectangle we apologize for. It needs enough footprint for browsing, sitting, and at least one weird corner that feels found rather than assigned.

Pipeline note: four fully furnished prefab rooms is a lot for pre-alpha if each one needs custom interactables, custom collision exceptions, and custom lighting rules.

I like the contract. I do not like accidentally promising four production rooms when what we need is one believable loop.

My question is less “where are the rooms” and more “what is each room asking the player to become?”

Cafe says arrive. Library says linger. Game Room says invite. Conference Hall says… behave? attend? wait for a calendar invite? That last one feels like a room from another game unless we can name its promise.

Yes to both objections. The Library being visually subordinate is not intended, and Priya is right that “four quadrants” must not mean “four finished vertical slices.”

Maybe v2 should define four places in the clubhouse, but only Cafe + Library need to feel authored for the next milestone. Game Room can be sturdy and sparse. Conference Hall can be a door we believe in more than a room we finish.

Center works. Door sightlines are clean.

Library needs depth. Two-tile threshold feels like storage closet.

Unblocking question for scope:

  • Do we need all four quadrants traversable in the pre-alpha build?
  • Do we need all four furnished?
  • Which room do we want a first-time demo viewer to remember after five minutes?

I can plan around any answer, but those are three different schedules pretending to be one map decision.

I am wildly into the prefab doorway contract because it means I can stop writing “if player is in east-ish room maybe fade music??” nonsense at 2am.

room.kind = "library"
room.entry = "south"
room.mood = "quiet"

Fake code, real relief!

Very rough napkin pass. Please enjoy the professional margin note where I wrote “book chunk??” and then apparently gave up.

I tried enlarging the Library north-west and letting its shelves bite slightly into the lobby silhouette. It makes the lobby less perfectly symmetric, which I think is good? Perfect symmetry can feel like a mall directory.

Possibly basic question: if rooms are prefabs, can the Library be bigger than the other quadrants without breaking the doorway contract?

Or is the contract only about entrances and tile alignment, not the full rectangle size?

Not basic, that is the actual question.

Doorway contract can be fixed while room bounds vary. What gets expensive is nav stitching, camera framing, and spawn safety if every room invents its own geometry. A larger Library is fine if it still obeys the same north/south/east/west attachment points.

Repro steps for player confusion in current long-clubhouse layout:

  1. Spawn in lobby.
  2. Walk east looking for “the social place.”
  3. Enter a room that visually reads as final.
  4. Assume the rest of the clubhouse is optional or hidden.

Expected: central lobby communicates there are multiple equal destinations.

Actual: hallway says “keep walking until content happens.”

Four-quadrant shape likely fixes this.

I want to underline Tomas’s asymmetry point. If the Library presses into the lobby a little, it becomes a neighbor instead of a category.

Visually: Cafe can glow outward, Game Room can spill motion, Library can have mass. Bookshelves are our friend here. A larger footprint is not just more tiles; it changes the read from “quiet room” to “place with gravity.”

For Library narrative purpose: I can write toward “the place where the clubhouse remembers.” Not lore dump, not quest board, not collectible museum. More like a soft archive of people having been here.

That supports the larger footprint. Memory needs corners.

“Memory needs corners” is going directly into the map doc unless you stop me.

I am convinced on bigger Library. The remaining tension is Conference Hall. I put it in because we need somewhere for future group events to live, but Ingrid’s “behave?” read is painful because it is accurate.

Conference Hall can be a closed double door for now.

Animation cost: small. Mystery value: medium. Risk of empty room: zero.

Closed double door also lets us test “future room exists” without building the future room. I can put an interaction stub on it that says something neutral like “Not set up yet” but hopefully less haunted.

Schedule read if we do that:

  • Four-quadrant shell: yes for pre-alpha.
  • Cafe: furnished enough for arrival/social proof.
  • Library: enlarged and furnished enough to feel intentional.
  • Game Room: blocked in, minimal props.
  • Conference Hall: exterior/door only, no interior promise beyond “later.”

This feels like a build we can actually show without apologizing every thirty seconds.
That schedule is shippable if “Library enlarged” lands this week before furnishing starts. Resizing after props go in is the part where everyone hears my keyboard from three timezones away.
I can do a cleaner Library-larger sketch tonight my time. Not promising beauty, promising rectangles with intent.

Rectangles with intent are my preferred genre.

One more art-direction ask: please do not make the enlarged Library a square. It should have a reading pocket or alcove so the footprint says “stay” before any prop art arrives.

content:
I like where this landed. The map has a clear thesis now: the lobby is the shared heart, the rooms are distinct invitations, and we are not pretending future scope is present scope.

Proceed with four quadrants. Enlarge the Library. Park the Conference Hall’s purpose until we know what kind of gathering Common Room actually earns.

Decision captured:

  • Keep the four-quadrant layout around the central lobby.
  • Increase Library footprint and give it at least one alcove/pocket before furnishing.
  • Treat Conference Hall as a future-purpose exterior for now, not a furnished pre-alpha room.
  • Prefab pipeline proceeds with fixed doorway contracts and variable room bounds.

Thank you for making the map sharper instead of just bigger. This thread is the game, honestly.

title: World Map v2 design spec
content:
World Map v2 is the first layout I am comfortable calling a spec instead of a sketch. It keeps the clubhouse readable at 16x16 tiles, gives each activity room a job, and stops treating the lobby like leftover hallway glue. The goal is still simple: a player should know where they are within two seconds, understand where their friends might be, and have enough small friction to make crossing the building feel like being in a place.

Top-Level Shape

The clubhouse remains a four-quadrant map around a central lobby. We tried the long-house layout in Playtest 14 and it made everyone walk in straight lines like they were late for a dentist appointment. The quadrant layout gives us better sightlines, easier mental mapping, and more excuses for little threshold moments.

NW Cafe        | NE Library Nook
--------------+--------------
      Central Lobby
--------------+--------------
SW Game Room   | SE Conference Hall

All rooms are built on the 16x16 tile grid. Door openings should land on even tile coordinates where possible so pathing, rugs, and wall trims do not fight the editor. Please do not freehand half-tile visual tricks in the level editor unless we have a matching collision story. Pretty lies become bug reports.

Room Dimensions

Central Lobby

  • Size: 24 tiles wide by 18 tiles tall.
  • Purpose: orientation, arrivals, soft idle, and the shortest route between all rooms.
  • Entrances: one 4-tile opening per quadrant, centered on the room edge.

The lobby gets the announcement board on the north wall, mail cubbies on the west side, and the big round rug in the center. Keep the rug walkable. People naturally gather on it, and when we blocked the middle with decor in Playtest 15, the whole lobby became a furniture apology.

NW Cafe

  • Size: 22 by 18 tiles.
  • Primary loop: order, sit, emote, overhear proximity chatter.
  • Key props: counter, pastry case, two booth clusters, window plants, dish return.

The cafe should feel busy without being dense. We need at least two paths from the door to the counter so four players can enter without making a polite traffic jam. Tables should create pockets, not traps. Two-tile chair clearance stays mandatory.

NE Library Nook

  • Size: 26 by 20 tiles.
  • RFC decision: enlarged from 20 by 16 after the reading circle test.
  • Primary loop: browse shelves, sit quietly together, pin notes, find hidden book prompts.

This is the biggest v2 change. The RFC feedback was consistent: the library was loved, but it did not have enough air. When three players sat near the fireplace, everyone else felt like they were intruding on a private conversation. The larger room lets us keep the cozy density around the hearth while adding a second calm zone by the tall shelves.

Library shelves should be chunky and legible in pixel art. Favor 2-tile-deep shelf runs with readable endcaps. The level editor should tag shelves as browseable, not just decorative, so interaction prompts remain consistent when we reshuffle books later.

SW Game Room

  • Size: 24 by 20 tiles.
  • Primary loop: short table games, spectating, cheering, wandering between activities.
  • Key props: board game table, arcade cabinet pair, puzzle wall, beanbags, snack crate.

The game room can tolerate more noise, but it still cannot become a casino floor. The arcade machines face inward so their animations do not steal attention from the lobby entrance. The board game table gets a 6 by 6 footprint, with one tile of clearance on all sides for spectators. If this feels generous, good. Spectating is part of play.

SE Conference Hall

  • Size: 26 by 18 tiles, blocked out only.
  • RFC decision: parked for now.
  • Status: collision-safe shell, minimal props, no dedicated mechanics in v2.

The conference hall stays in the map because the clubhouse needs the architectural balance, but we are not building a full meeting feature for this milestone. The RFC did its job here: several of us had a version of this room in our heads, and none of those versions were the same room. Rather than ship a vague multipurpose box, we park it as a clean shell with stackable chairs, a folded stage, and one locked storage door for future trouble.

Parked means intentionally boring, not neglected. Make it tidy, plausible, and cheap to revise.

Lobby Hub Rules

  1. From the spawn tile, all four room entrances must be visible or strongly implied by wall openings.
  2. No decorative object in the lobby may reduce the main cross path below 3 walkable tiles.
  3. Each quadrant entrance gets a distinct floor transition: cafe checker, library runner, game room carpet strip, conference polished wood.
  4. Ambient animations should be strongest at room edges, not in the center of the lobby.

The lobby is also our main async metaphor, whether or not players notice it. You can pass through, pause, leave a note, spot a friend, or drift into a room without the game asking for commitment. That is Common Room in miniature.

Implementation Notes

Please build v2 in the level editor as clubhouse_world_v2, not by mutating the current playtest map. We need side-by-side comparison for the next session. Use tile layer names exactly as below so tools do not need another round of guessing.

floor_base
floor_transitions
walls_collision
walls_trim
props_solid
props_soft
interactables
spawn_points
ambient_markers

Spawn points should remain in the lobby: one primary at the south edge of the rug, three alternates along the west mail cubbies. Do not spawn players directly inside activity rooms yet. That made the world feel efficient and weird, like teleporting into a chair.

Playtest Targets

  • Players can find the cafe, library, and game room without map UI.
  • Two groups of three can occupy the library without feeling like one group is blocking the other.
  • Four players crossing the lobby in opposite directions do not snag on props.
  • The conference hall reads as closed-but-real, not missing content.

I want the next playtest to answer one question cleanly: does the clubhouse feel bigger without becoming slower? If yes, we lock the shell and start pushing room-specific interactions. If no, we trim distance before adding toys.

title: beginner question: how does tile collision actually work here?
content:
I am trying to follow the clubhouse movement code and I think I understand the player bounds part, but I keep getting fuzzy on how the collision layers map back to the 16x16 tile grid.

Very basic version of my question: when a player walks into the cafe counter, are we checking the exact pixels of the sprite, the tile the sprite's feet are on, or some collision layer exported from the map? I can see collision in the Tiled export and I can see the movement code snapping to tile coords, but my brain has not connected those two things yet.

Asking here instead of quietly making a cursed diagram in my notebook because I suspect future me will need the answer too.

Good question, and yes, this is the exact place where the code looks more magical than it is.

We do not collide against the visible sprite pixels. We collide against a small rectangle near the player's feet, then ask which 16x16 tiles that rectangle overlaps. Those tile coords are used to look up the collision layer from the map export.

const left = Math.floor(feetRect.left / 16)
const right = Math.floor((feetRect.right - 1) / 16)
const top = Math.floor(feetRect.top / 16)
const bottom = Math.floor((feetRect.bottom - 1) / 16)

for each tile in that range:
  if collisionLayer[tile.y][tile.x] === SOLID:
    rejectMove()

So for the cafe counter: art lives on the decorative layers, but the invisible collision layer says which grid cells are solid. The player sprite can visually overlap the counter a little because the feet rectangle is smaller than the full sprite. That is intentional so walking behind/near furniture feels less like pushing a cardboard cutout around.

title: Block out the four quadrants in the level editor
description:
Grey-box the cafe, library nook, game room, and conference hall around the central lobby using the v2 layout decision.

Keep it rough but playable on the 16x16 grid so pathing, sightlines, and hangout pockets are easy to judge.

content:
Adding one extra mental model because this helped me: the 16x16 grid is the authority, the art is persuasion.

The chair might be drawn with legs that only take up half a tile, but if the collision layer marks that tile solid, your feet box treats the whole tile as blocked. When we need softer behavior, like the library nook chairs where you can tuck in closer, we usually solve it by changing the collision shape/tiles in the map, not by sampling pixels.

Also the reason we check the swept next position instead of the current tile is so diagonal movement does not tunnel through a corner. Tiny math, large vibes!

That clicks. I was imagining the sprite bounds hitting the object art, which made the cafe counter behavior seem impossible. Feet rectangle plus collision layer is much cleaner.

I am going to add a tiny debug overlay locally that draws the feet rect and the occupied tile coords while I step through this. Not committing it unless it turns out useful, but I need to see the boxes once or my brain will keep inventing fake physics.

title: Prefab / room pipeline plan (post world-map decision)
content:
World-map v2 gives us the rule I wanted: rooms are destinations, not free-floating scenes. So the room pipeline can stay boring.

Proposal:

world_map.json
  nodes: [
    { id: "lobby", prefab: "rooms/lobby", spawn: "front_door" },
    { id: "cafe", prefab: "rooms/cafe", spawn: "counter" },
    { id: "library_nook", prefab: "rooms/library_nook", spawn: "sofa" }
  ]

loadRoom(nodeId) {
  const node = worldMap.nodes[nodeId]
  const prefab = prefabRegistry.get(node.prefab)

  room.clear()
  room.mount(prefab.layers)
  room.spawnPlayers(prefab.spawns[node.spawn])
  room.attachInteractables(prefab.interactables)
}

Prefabs should be data-first, not scene-first. A room prefab owns tile layers, collision, spawn points, interactables, ambient tags. The world map owns unlock/routing/state. No room should know where it sits in the map.

Initial boundaries:

  • Prefab id: stable path-ish key, ex. rooms/cafe
  • Room instance id: map node id, ex. cafe or conference_hall_after_hours
  • Spawn key: named point inside prefab, ex. front_door
  • Interactables: prefab-local ids namespaced at runtime as {roomInstanceId}:{localId}

This gives us cheap reuse later. Same prefab, different map node, different flags.

runtimeInteractableId = `${roomInstanceId}:${interactable.localId}`
// cafe:board_menu
// cafe_after_party:board_menu

I want to ship the first pass with JSON manifests and our current tilemap exporter. If this starts turning into archaeology, we stop and build the missing tool instead of inventing ceremonies.

title: ✨ we have custom emojis now ✨ (meet the mascot)
content:
tiny but important office news: Gameplan now has our first custom emoji set, pulled from Common Room badge art.

please welcome:

  • {{emoji:cozy}} for warm yes energy
  • {{emoji:rug}} for soft landing / general room feelings
  • {{emoji:moonhollow}} for studio pride without making a whole speech
  • {{emoji:playtest}} for brave souls entering the build
  • {{emoji:espresso}} for the cafe espresso machine, which is still one tile too tall and therefore has been promoted to official mascot

discord has already asked for “the tall coffee friend” twice, so this feels correct. use irresponsibly but with taste.

Approved office infrastructure.

  • {{emoji:playtest}} is going directly into the Friday build thread.
  • {{emoji:cozy}} solves at least three different “I read this and support it but have no new information” replies.
  • {{emoji:espresso}} has the posture of someone who knows where the merge conflict is.

One actual question: do we want the same set exported for Discord this week, or keep Gameplan first and see what people reach for?

Yes to boring. Tooling ask: can the manifest be the source of truth and the exporter only writes it, not secretly reorders/renames things? The CI is my garden and hidden editor behavior is invasive mint.

I want a validator early:

validatePrefab(prefab) {
  assertUnique(prefab.spawns.map(s => s.id))
  assertUnique(prefab.interactables.map(i => i.localId))
  assertLayerExists(prefab.layers, "collision")
  assertNoRuntimeIds(prefab.interactables)
}

Also please leave a clean seam for a tiny room preview tool. Not a whole editor yet. Just load prefab, show collision/spawns/interactables, fail loudly when the JSON is cursed.

{{emoji:espresso}} {{emoji:espresso}} {{emoji:espresso}}

I am not saying this will improve my 2am progress gifs, but I am saying the espresso machine looks like it would approve of them!!!

The badge treatment is doing the right thing here: chunky border, readable silhouette, no tiny interior noise. The espresso machine being slightly too vertical is exactly why it works at emoji size. If we “fix” the height, it becomes furniture. If we leave it tall, it becomes a character.

So yes, mascot status is visually defensible.

Naming question: should roomInstanceId be allowed to differ from the visible room name forever? Like conference_hall_after_hours can still display as “Conference Hall”? I think yes, but asking before I put the wrong word in comments and haunt future-me.

Bug report:

  1. Open thread.
  2. Observe new custom emoji.
  3. Attempt to respond normally.
  4. Fail.
  5. Send {{emoji:espresso}} instead.

Expected: professional restraint. Actual: mascot.

Instance id separate from display name feels right to me! Especially if we do seasonal or event-y variants without duplicating all the art. Also this makes net sync cleaner because the client can say “I am in cafe_after_party” and not care what the UI calls it.

roomState = {
  instanceId: "cafe_after_party",
  prefabId: "rooms/cafe",
  displayNameKey: "room.cafe.name"
}

I am trying very hard not to make this bigger than it is. Medium success.

the espresso machine has the energy of a tiny brass section warming up in the cafe corner.

too tall, slightly serious, probably making eye contact with the pastry case.

title: Implement prefab / room instancing pipeline
description:
Build the prefab pipeline that world-map v2 needs for room-sized chunks like the cafe, library nook, game room, and conference hall.

Keep the format friendly to source control so tiny tile fixes do not turn into mystery blobs.

content:
Yep: instanceId is stable runtime identity, displayNameKey is UI. Let’s use Marcus’s shape.

Agree with Zoe on validator-first. I’ll add:

  • prefab manifest schema + loader
  • validator in CI for duplicate ids / missing collision / missing spawn target

@Zoe Tran can you take the preview harness after that exists? No editor promises, just “show me what the engine thinks this room is.”

Need spawn point rotation too. Not just position.

spawns: {
  front_door: { x: 8, y: 14, facing: "north" }
}

Animation entry reads facing. Otherwise first frame pops.

Discord export is easy, so I’ll prep it after lunch and keep Gameplan as the source of truth for names. Also reporting that “professional restraint / actual: mascot” has already been copied into the community notes doc because that is exactly the kind of sentence our playtesters will steal.

{{emoji:moonhollow}} {{emoji:espresso}}

I’ll take preview harness once the schema lands. It can be ugly as long as it is honest. Famous last words, but typed with discipline.

Suggested tasks:

  • Priya: prefab manifest schema, loader, CI validator
  • Zoe: room preview harness with layer toggles for collision/spawns/interactables

And +1 to Kenji’s facing. If the first thing our cozy room loader does is rotate everyone like confused chess pieces, we deserve the bug reports.

Locked then:

  • prefabId = reusable asset/data package
  • instanceId = world-map runtime node
  • displayNameKey = UI string
  • spawn.facing is required
  • validator ships with the loader, preview harness follows

I’ll file the two tasks and start with the schema. If the JSON starts feeling like a tiny database, we pause and complain in a new thread like professionals.

title: Settling the lobby rug once and for all (poll)
content:
I am invoking producer privilege and making the lobby rug debate measurable, because yesterday's art pass thread somehow became 37% rug.

Poll is up: Which lobby rug?

  • Woven geometric
  • Round braided jute
  • Deep Persian-ish
  • No rug (heresy)

The actual unblocking question: what choice best supports first-read clarity when a new player spawns into the lobby and is deciding whether to go cafe, library nook, game room, or conference hall?

Strong opinions welcome. Threats against rugs should be phrased as actionable feedback.

Woven geometric is the only one that gives us a soft compass without drawing a literal compass on the floor, which I will go on record as opposing until my final breath.

The lobby's job is to say: this place branches, but you are not lost. A subtle rectangular weave under the central seating group can point north/south/east/west through pattern language, while still feeling like something the clubhouse bought because somebody had taste. Round jute feels friendly but makes the lobby read like a cafe waiting area. Persian-ish is beautiful but it says old-money parlor, and I do not want players to wonder whether they need to take their shoes off before chatting.

No rug is a brave act of violence against warmth.

I am voting round braided jute and accepting whatever legal consequences follow.

The central lobby already has strong orthogonal language from the tile grid, benches, notice board, and four exits. A rectangle rug doubles down on that. The round braided one breaks the grid just enough to make the spawn area feel gathered rather than assigned. It also gives Tomas room to do a little frayed edge treatment at 16x16 without turning the pattern into visual noise.

Deep Persian-ish is a no from me for palette reasons. It steals red from the cafe awning and makes the lobby look like it is wearing the cafe's jacket.

I made tiny scratch sketches of all four and the rude answer is that "no rug" makes the lobby floor look clean in a way that is maybe too honest.

Woven geometric reads best at zoom. Round jute reads best in my heart. Persian-ish reads as "Tomas discovered a new dithering brush and should be stopped."

Engineering note that nobody asked for: any rug with a strong border is going to make players think there is collision or an interaction hotspot unless we keep the edge very soft!!!

I voted woven because the repeated pattern can imply "decor" instead of "trigger zone." Also because I refuse to debug a bug titled "player cannot sit on rug" when the problem is philosophical.

Discord data point, from the cozy-game lurker trenches: players routinely describe spaces as "welcoming" when there is some kind of textile in the entrance. Blank floor gets called clean, tidy, polished, etc. Rug gets called homey.

That said, the no-rug voters are going to be extremely loud and extremely funny, so I support keeping that option in the poll for community health. 😂

Possibly silly question: do we need the rug to mark spawn because players arrive there, or do we want spawn to feel less marked? I voted no rug because I kept noticing the rug before the exits, but that might be because I knew we were arguing about it.

Repro steps:

  1. Spawn in lobby.
  2. Observe rug.
  3. Ask whether rug is decoration, navigation, seating area, interaction area, or emotional load-bearing infrastructure.
  4. Fail to proceed to cafe.

Expected: rug clarifies space.

Actual: rug discourse enters second week.

Voted no rug for isolation purposes.

title: Enlarge the library nook footprint
description:
Apply the agreed library resize so the nook has room for more shelves and a proper window-seat cluster.

Please keep the entrance cozy without turning it into a hallway with books.

content:
From a tooling perspective, "no rug" has the strongest asset pipeline story: zero export mistakes, zero atlas churn, zero naming debate. Truly elegant.

From a human perspective, I hate that it might be right.

title: Prefab / Room Pipeline
content:
This is the current map pipeline for Common Room: how we author reusable prefabs, turn them into room instances, and get them into a playtest build without anyone hand-editing coordinates at midnight. The goal is boring reliability. Pixel art is already enough tiny squares to argue with.

Terms

  • Tile: one 16x16 grid cell. All collision, nav, and placement data snaps to this.
  • Prefab: a reusable chunk authored in the level editor. Example: seat_pair_round_table, library_window_bay_03, cafe_counter_corner_ne.
  • Room: an instanced area with bounds, exits, spawn anchors, ambient zones, and prefab references.
  • Quadrant: the big four around the lobby: cafe, library nook, game room, conference hall.

Authoring Flow

  1. Build or edit the prefab in the level editor.
  2. Save into assets/rooms/prefabs/<quadrant>/.
  3. Run validation from the editor toolbar before committing.
  4. Reference the prefab from a room file by id, not path.
  5. Load the room in the local playtest harness and walk the edges.

That last step is not optional. Validation catches structure; walking catches “two players can stand inside the ficus if they approach from the west.” The validator is a seatbelt, not a designer.

Prefab Files

Prefab ids are stable API. Rename the file if you must, but do not change the id unless you are also migrating every room reference. The editor writes these fields:

{
  "id": "cafe_table_two_top_01",
  "tile_size": 16,
  "bounds": { "w": 4, "h": 3 },
  "layers": ["floor_overlay", "props", "collision"],
  "anchors": {
    "sit_left": [0, 1],
    "sit_right": [3, 1]
  },
  "tags": ["cafe", "seating", "social"]
}

Anchors are named for gameplay intent, not art direction. Use sit_left, not cute_chair. Future us cannot query vibes.

Naming Conventions

  • <quadrant>_<object>_<variant> for quadrant-specific art.
  • shared_<object>_<variant> for objects that belong anywhere, like rugs and lamps.
  • Use compass suffixes for directional pieces: _n, _se, _nw.
  • Use two digits for art variants: library_shelf_tall_02.
  • Do not encode coordinates in names. If the name has x12_y08, please delete it with kindness.

Room Instances

Rooms are light. They define bounds, quadrant ownership, exits, music cue, ambient crowd budget, and prefab placements. A room does not duplicate tile data from a prefab. At load time we resolve prefab ids from the registry, stamp layers into the room buffer, then build collision and nav from the merged result.

{
  "id": "clubhouse_cafe",
  "quadrant": "cafe",
  "origin": [0, 0],
  "bounds": { "w": 32, "h": 24 },
  "prefabs": [
    { "id": "cafe_counter_corner_ne", "at": [2, 3] },
    { "id": "cafe_table_two_top_01", "at": [10, 12] }
  ]
}

Load Order

  1. Load room manifest.
  2. Resolve prefab ids through the generated registry.
  3. Stamp visual layers from floor up.
  4. Merge collision masks.
  5. Derive nav cells and soft blockers.
  6. Attach interaction anchors.
  7. Emit debug overlays if ROOM_DEBUG=1.

Collision wins over vibes. If a chair has a sit anchor but the collision layer blocks that tile, the loader rejects the room. This is intentional because half-working seating is how we get playtest notes like “I became table.”

Level Editor Contract

The editor is the source of truth for prefab geometry. Code is the source of truth for loader behavior. The editor may preview nav, collision, and anchors, but it should not implement a second copy of the room loader. It calls the same validation package used in CI.

If the editor and game disagree, the game is right and the editor needs to show the disagreement louder.

Current editor hooks:

  • onPrefabSave writes normalized JSON and regenerates the local registry.
  • onRoomOpen resolves prefab ids and shows missing references in red.
  • onValidate runs bounds, anchor, collision, nav, and naming checks.
  • onPlaytest launches directly into the selected room with two bot clients parked near exits.

Playtest Checks

Before a room goes into a Friday build, check these in the harness:

  • Two players can pass each other at every door and lobby transition.
  • No sit anchor is inside collision or facing a wall unless it is a wall bench.
  • The cafe counter does not trap players behind staff-only tiles. We have done this twice. Enough.
  • Library nook paths still work with players idling near shelves.
  • Game room tables support approach from at least two sides.
  • Conference hall rows do not create one-tile choke points near the entrance.

CI Validation

CI runs roomlint on every prefab and room file. It checks schema, id uniqueness, tile alignment, missing prefabs, anchors outside bounds, duplicate anchors, and impossible nav islands. It does not check whether something looks welcoming. That remains our burden and also the fun part.

pnpm roomlint assets/rooms
pnpm room-preview clubhouse_cafe --debug-collision
pnpm playtest:room clubhouse_conference_hall --clients=4

When adding a new quadrant prefab, keep the first version small and boring. Ship library_reading_chair_01, use it in one room, watch a playtest, then make the fancy version. The pipeline is fastest when we let prefabs earn complexity through use.

title: Library nook mood board (making the case for more space)
content:
I put together the library nook mood board with the current map crop beside it, and I’m going to be gentle but very stubborn here: the nook needs a little more room than we’re giving it.

Right now it reads as “bookshelf corner.” What I think Common Room wants is “I meant to pass through the lobby and somehow sat down for twenty minutes.” That needs a window seat, a lamp pool, at least one deep shelf face, and enough negative space for two players to idle without looking like they’re blocking the aisle.

I’m not asking for a whole new wing. I am asking that the reading corner stop being the first thing we shave down when the map gets tight.

Tiny ugly sketch, but: if we bump the shelves back one tile and let the window seat make the corner, the whole thing suddenly has somewhere to breathe.

books books books
[ ] lamp   seat
    rug?   window

Please imagine this with 100% more charm and 80% fewer rectangles.

This is the first version where I can picture the library nook as a place people return to, not just a themed wall. The window seat also gives us a lovely fiction handle: it can be the spot where club members leave borrowed books for each other, which makes the room feel shared without adding mechanics.

Producer hat: I’m convinced. The unblocking question is whether the resize pulls from the conference hall edge or from the central lobby buffer.

  • I’ll add Lena’s board and Tomas’s sketch note to the resize task.
  • Can we try one pass that keeps the lobby path clean for demo movement?
  • If that works, this feels like the right trade.

Poll closed and I regret to inform everyone that democracy has produced a dead tie between Woven geometric and No rug.

So: tabling the rug for now. We will ship the lobby blockout with the floor neutral, keep Tomas's woven sketch in the parking lot, and revisit after the next fresh-eyes playtest when people are reacting to the whole room instead of our very normal rug court transcript.

The rug remains gloriously unresolved, exactly as feared.

title: Furnish the cafe quadrant
description:
Dress the cafe with the counter run, two seating clusters, a few plants, and the espresso machine that has decided it is architecture.

title: RFC: build cadence for the next month
content:
I want to move us to a weekly internal build cadence for the next month while we head into the furnishing sprint.

Proposal:

cut: Thursdays, 18:00 UTC
branch: main
label: internal-YYYY-MM-DD
scope: playable clubhouse loop + whatever furnishings are merged
notes: 5 bullets max in Releases

Why weekly: the demo is going to feel better or worse based on accumulation, not individual PRs. I want us catching “the cafe feels empty when three people idle there” and “chair placement is weird at the library table” while those things are still cheap.

Not proposing ceremony. Just a reliable build, quick smoke pass, and a thread for breakage. If Thursday is cursed by CI, Friday morning UTC is the escape hatch.

I can wire the label + artifact naming so it is boring in the good way. The CI is my garden and Thursday cuts are acceptable weather.

Mechanics question: do we want the smoke pass to block the label, or do we label the build first and mark smoke status in the release note?

Label first, smoke status in the release note feels right to me. Otherwise we make the build cut depend on whoever is awake, which gets silly fast.

  • Thursday build cut by Zoe/tooling
  • Friday async smoke pass in the release thread
  • Demo-viewer notes stay separate unless they are blocking

One unblocking question: are we expecting every weekly build to be multiplayer-smoked with at least two clients, or only the ones where lobby/cafe/library sync touched code?

I vote every weekly build gets a two-client smoke, but tiny: join room, move between lobby and one side room, place one furnishing if that code is present, leave cleanly. Ten minutes, not a doctoral thesis.

Approved. Weekly internal builds, label first, smoke status in the thread, two-client smoke kept deliberately small.

This gives us a steadier read on the demo without turning Fridays into a ritual. Thank you.

title: What is the conference hall actually FOR?
content:
Pulling one unanswered thread from the world-map pass: the conference hall is still a room we are drawing beautifully around, but not yet a room with a reason to exist.

Right now it reads as “large civic rectangle,” which is funny but also dangerous. The cafe says linger. The library nook says quiet company. The game room says low-stakes play. The conference hall says... quarterly all-hands? Please no.

My early lore brain keeps trying to rescue it with events: watch-parties for tiny in-world broadcasts, community screenings, maybe a rotating “town reel” where players can sit together while something short and ambient happens on the big wall. I like the idea that the clubhouse inherited a too-formal space and the residents softened it into a shared watching/listening room. Less boardroom, more borrowed auditorium with mismatched chairs.

But if we keep it, I think it needs a verb. “Gather” is too broad. “Attend” is too stiff. “Watch together” might be enough, but I am not sure yet.

No decision needed from this post. I just do not want us to arrive at the next playable map and have someone ask, very reasonably, why the biggest room is also the most mysterious.

My instinct is that the danger is not the room being formal, it is the room being legible as important without giving players permission to do anything there.

I am very into “watch together” as a candidate verb because it gives us a social behavior that is passive on purpose. Cafe chatting is active. Game room is active. Library is parallel presence. A screening space could be the place where the game says: you can sit beside someone and let the room carry the moment.

That said, the implementation trap is making it feel like a content delivery machine. If players walk in and expect a schedule, a queue, rewards, announcements, etc., we have accidentally built a theater system instead of a cozy room. I would rather it be occasional, environmental, almost optional. The big wall can be off most of the time and still make sense if the room has enough social affordances when nothing is playing.

Unblocking question for the map: does the conference hall need to prove itself in the first playtest, or can it be intentionally under-specified as long as it is not confusing?

  • If it needs to prove itself: we should pick a placeholder interaction now.
  • If it can stay soft: we should make sure the room does not look like the main objective hub.
  • Either way, “biggest room with least clarity” is a real risk for observation notes.

Discord brain says people will absolutely make it “movie night room” within five minutes, even if we never call it that. Which is kind of lovely? But also means if there is a huge blank wall and rows of chairs, they will ask where the movie button is. 😅

title: the sound of a room that's just... warm
content:
i keep coming back to this: common room should sound like a warm room you don't want to leave.

not busy. not sleepy. somewhere between kettle-on and rain-at-the-window, with enough little life in the walls that silence never feels empty.

the cafe can clink and breathe, the library can hush without scolding, the game room can laugh in the corners, and the conference hall can have that soft carpeted patience of a room waiting for friends to arrive.

i want the music to feel like it noticed you came in, then made space for you. melodies that don't tug on your sleeve. textures that make idling feel intentional. the kind of loop where, after ten minutes, you realize your shoulders dropped.

if we get it right, leaving the clubhouse should feel less like closing an app and more like stepping out of a place that's still glowing behind you.

title: Why we're building Common Room
content:
I want to name something before the month gets loud.

Common Room exists because we believe a small game can make people feel less alone for ten minutes at a time. Not through pressure, not through grind, not through a hundred systems asking to be fed. Through a place that remembers you were there. Through a chair by the window. Through a friend waving from the library nook. Through a room that feels safe enough to be quiet in.

We are making that game on our own terms, with people who like each other, at a pace that lets the work keep its shape. That is not a side benefit. It is the point.

So as we head into this next stretch, please protect the thing underneath the tasks. Be specific. Be kind. Say the true thing early. Make the pixel honest. Leave room for someone in Tokyo or Toronto or San Francisco to answer when their day starts.

If we do that well, the game will know.

Thank you for putting this where we can point back to it.

  • Specific.
  • Kind.
  • True thing early.
  • Leave room for the person whose morning has not started yet.

That is basically the operating manual I want for demo month.

Yes, that is the exact haunted sentence: where is the movie button.

I am going to keep noodling on “a room that remembers gatherings” rather than “a room that runs events.” Maybe the lore answer is older than the mechanic, and the mechanic stays extremely light until we know whether players want to occupy it or just decode it.

This lands hard for me because the design temptation, especially right now, is to prove the game is enough by adding more proof. More verbs, more reward loops, more little hooks so no one can accuse the room of being empty.

But the promise is not that Common Room has infinite things to do. The promise is that being there feels cared for. A table placed one tile to the left because it makes the path feel welcoming is not smaller work than a new feature. It is the work.

I am going to keep repeating this to myself when I get itchy.

Keeping this close today. Also gently adding the people-ops footnote: making the game on our own terms includes resting on our own terms. If your PTO has been sitting there looking decorative, this is a good week to put a date on it.

title: Audio direction for the warm room
content:
common room should sound like a place that has already been loved for a while. not old in a dusty way, not precious, just gently inhabited: mugs set down on wood, rain worrying at the windows, a kettle that clicks off in the cafe, someone crossing the lobby in soft shoes. the pixel art is 16x16 and the sound needs to honor that scale. every sound should feel close enough to touch, but never so busy that it crowds the chat.

the center idea

our audio direction is warmth through small evidence. we are not scoring adventure, danger, progress, or achievement. we are scoring presence. when a player logs in from san francisco at breakfast and another arrives from tokyo near midnight, the room should hold both of them without announcing either as the main character.

if the screen says shared clubhouse, the speakers should say someone left a lamp on for you.

this means we bias toward soft transients, short tails, and textures with air in them. the mix should feel handmade but not lo-fi for its own sake. we are making a room, not a cassette filter.

global bed: the room tone

there should be one very subtle global layer across the whole clubhouse: a barely-there vinyl crackle and felted room tone that ties the quadrants together. i know crackle can become a shortcut. the intent here is not nostalgia wallpaper. it is a continuity thread, like the hum of a building.

  • default volume should sit below conscious attention. in playtests, if three people mention it unprompted in the first minute, it is too loud.

  • the layer should duck slightly under ui pings, chat send sounds, and proximity emotes.

  • it should vary by time of day, with a softer high-frequency edge during late-night palettes.

  • it must be mutable in accessibility settings as ambience, not music.

for implementation, i want this as a single global bus with tiny randomized grains rather than a looping file we all slowly learn to hate.

global_room_bed
  crackle: sparse, irregular, -38 to -42 db
  hum: soft mid-low, no obvious pitch center
  ducking: ui + proximity + music stem
  setting: ambience volume

quadrant ambience

central lobby

the lobby is the blend point. it should not have the strongest identity, because players cross it constantly. light cloth movement, wood floor ticks, a distant radiator, maybe a little glass rattle when rain is active. footsteps here can be the reference tone for the rest of the map.

cafe

the cafe gets the warmest transient palette: ceramic, kettle, low counter taps, espresso steam softened so it never hisses like an alarm. chairs should have tiny felt pads in the foley, even if the sprite suggests bare wood. our cafe is not a productivity cafe. it is a place to sit badly and stay.

library nook

paper, cushions, page turns, pencil on index card, rain if the weather system is active. no aggressive shushing. the library should be quiet but not brittle. if two players idle here, the ambience should make silence feel intentional instead of empty.

game room

this quadrant can have the most playful edges, but they need restraint. plastic button clicks, a soft arcade cabinet fan, board game pieces in a small tray, a deck of cards being squared. no casino sparkle. no victory sting unless the minigame asks for it directly.

conference hall

slightly larger air, fabric banners, the soft thump of a projector cart, distant folding-chair metal. this is where we host studio-wide rituals and playtest debriefs, so it should feel ready without feeling formal. the room tone can be wider here, but the center must stay clear for voice-like ui sounds.

music behavior

music should arrive like someone joining a conversation, not like a level beginning. short motifs, low repetition pressure, mostly diatonic but with a few suspended notes that keep the room breathing. stems can respond to population, but never in a way that punishes being alone. one player in the clubhouse still deserves the warm version.

  1. base stem: felt piano or soft electric keys, sparse enough to leave gaps.

  2. texture stem: brushed percussion, quiet tape flutter, or room percussion from cafe objects.

  3. presence stem: added only when three or more players are nearby, kept below the base melody.

level editor rules

the level editor needs audio zones that snap cleanly to the 16x16 grid, but the transitions should not sound tiled. designers should be able to paint cafe, library, game room, conference, lobby, and neutral zones. every placed prop with a sound source needs a radius preview, because otherwise we will fill the room with tiny kettles screaming at each other.

for prop loops, default to silence unless the sound tells the player something useful about the space. a lamp does not need a hum. a fish tank does. a vending machine maybe does, but only if we decide it belongs in the fiction and not because the sprite is cute.

playtest questions

  • do players lower the ambience slider before we ask about audio?

  • can they name which quadrant they are in with eyes closed after thirty seconds?

  • does the vinyl layer feel like warmth, dirt, or a bug?

  • during a busy thread discussion in gameplan, do ui sounds still read without becoming bossy?

  • when nobody talks for a minute, does the room feel calm or abandoned?

the best version of this audio is almost embarrassingly modest. it should make players linger, not because the mix asks for attention, but because leaving would make the room a little quieter.

title: Cafe quadrant, first furnished pass (WIP)
content:
First furnished pass for the cafe quadrant. Very WIP, especially clutter density and the north wall read, but the room is finally behaving like a place people would drift into instead of a diagram labeled CAFE.

Counter close-up below. I am still tuning value separation on the pastry case and register, and the chair backs may need one more pixel of kindness.
Tiny note from the corner: the espresso machine is one tile taller than the counter spec, but please consider this my formal vote that it stays as our extremely serious cafe mascot.
Counter stool idle has a 1px seam on frame 3 when it loops. Easy fix. Room works.

For demo planning, this is already doing a lot of heavy lifting.

  • The warm/cool split between cafe and lobby reads immediately.
  • The tables feel social without blocking pathing at a glance.
  • One question: should the pastry case be considered interactable for the July slice, or is it atmosphere only?
I love the table spacing!!! Also the tall espresso machine is going to make my collision brain complain for exactly five minutes and then I will name the fixture in code like it has tenure.

Cafe table by the plant is absolutely going to become somebody's Discord profile banner. Calling it now. The whole quadrant has that "I logged in to check one thing and accidentally sat down for 40 minutes" feeling.

What I like here is that it gives the cafe a reason to exist beyond being "the food corner." The counter creates a little implied queue, the small tables create side conversations, and the plant/table cluster gives us a soft edge into the lobby instead of a hard room boundary. That matters for Common Room because the map should keep inviting players to drift, not decide they are done with a room and leave it behind.

I would be careful with adding too much signage later. The furniture is already explaining the place.

Repro steps:

  1. Open image.
  2. Observe cafe.
  3. Attempt to remain professionally neutral.

Expected: QA restraint. Actual: whispered "oh no it's lovely" at desk.

Pastry case is atmosphere for July, unless design changes its mind and bribes me with a very small croissant verb. I will fix Kenji's stool seam and keep the espresso machine tall for now. It has posture.

title: Ship v1 of the in-house level editor
description:
Ship the first usable editor pass with a tile palette, prefab placement, and live collision preview for the 16x16 clubhouse maps.

Good enough means we can place chairs without reopening three tools and a prayer.

title: Fix walk-through collision on the cafe counter
description: The cafe counter collision mask is shifted one tile on the south edge, so players can clip through the bar during diagonal movement. Please fix the grid and sanity-check the stools while you are in that room.

title: BUG: player walks straight through the cafe counter
content:
Observed in today’s playtest build: player can walk through the cafe counter and end up embedded in the north wall. Filing before my brain tries to call this “emergent barista access.”

  1. Open Common Room build 0.18.4-playtest.7.
  2. Spawn in central lobby.
  3. Walk into the cafe from the south entrance.
  4. Stand one tile below the right half of the cafe counter.
  5. Hold northeast into the counter. I reproduced with keyboard, controller, and click-to-move. Speed does not matter: walk, slow nudge, or sprint all clip through.

Expected: player slides along the counter edge or stops.

Actual: player passes through the counter collision and lands inside the wall behind it. After that, horizontal movement still works but vertical movement gets sticky until respawn.

Repro rate: 10/10. Only found on the cafe counter so far, but I have not done a full collision sweep on the library shelves yet.
I can reproduce locally! It is very specifically the northeast push, which makes me suspect our diagonal normalization plus tile probe order is doing something rude. Taking first pass.
Question from the cheap seats: if speed does not matter, does that rule out tunneling? I thought tunneling was mostly when velocity skips over a collider between frames.
Correct. This does not look like high-speed tunneling. I can reproduce by tapping northeast one input frame at a time. It feels like the collision check believes the target tile is open.

I would start at the grid sample, not movement speed.

const right = Math.floor((next.x + halfWidth) / TILE_SIZE)
const top = Math.floor((next.y - halfHeight) / TILE_SIZE)

Counter is an object layer collider, wall is tile layer. If one of those converts max bounds differently, northeast is where we would notice.

Our collision overlay currently draws tile solids and object solids in two separate coordinate moods, because apparently one overlay was too easy. I am adding grid labels before we diagnose shadows on a cave wall.

First finding: the counter collider exists and is in the broadphase. The player is not ignoring it. The narrow check says “no overlap” for exactly one frame when the player’s right edge lands on the counter’s left edge. Then the next frame resolves against the wall, which is why you get pasted into it. Fun!

Unblocking question for demo planning: is this in the candidate branch for Thursday, or only in the internal playtest build?

  • If candidate branch: I will mark cafe route as risky for the guided demo.
  • If internal only: we can keep the demo script as-is and just track the fix.
Candidate branch is affected. I checked 0.18.4-rc.2 and reproduced there as well. Demo script currently walks past that counter at minute four, so I would treat this as blocking until fixed.

Overlay with grid labels, object bounds, and player bounds. The red counter box is one tile narrower than the art on its east edge. The player’s northeast corner fits through the lie.

Tooling note: this was not visible before because object-layer bounds were snapped for display but not labeled with their raw tile span. The CI is my garden and today it grows one more fence.

title: Proposal: naming convention for prefabs
content:
I keep losing the small prop variants in the level editor now that cafe, library, game room, and conference hall pieces are all in the same prefab browser. Proposal: we standardize prefab names before the browser turns into a junk drawer.

Suggested format:

Area_Category_Object_Variant_State

Examples:

Cafe_Furniture_Table_Round_Clean
Library_Prop_BookStack_Tall
GameRoom_Decor_Poster_Arcade_A
Shared_Lighting_Lamp_Table_On

Rules I think would help:

  • Area is the room or Shared if it appears in more than one room.

  • Category should be boring and searchable: Furniture, Decor, Lighting, Prop, Tile.

  • Variant is about visual difference, not placement.

  • State is for editor/runtime states like On, Off, Open, Closed.

If this seems reasonable, I can rename the existing prefabs in small batches so we do not break everyone's branches at once.

There it is. Object width from Tiled is inclusive in our importer, but we store it as exclusive everywhere else.

// current
right = left + width - 1

// should be
right = left + width

The counter is 4 tiles wide in art, 3 tiles wide in collision. Northeast finds the missing tile because top is already blocked by wall after the bad step.

I like this. Small tweak: make State optional and only use it when the prefab actually has an editor or runtime state. Otherwise we will grow a forest of Default suffixes.

Area_Category_Object_Variant
Area_Category_Object_Variant_State

Ship that as the rule and I will follow it in the lobby cleanup branch.
That explains why shelves did not fail the same way, right? Their object colliders are wider than the visible shelf art, so the off-by-one was hidden instead of making a gap?

Yep. Felix gets the prize: the bug was present in the importer, but only the cafe counter had art and collision aligned tightly enough to make it player-visible. Patch is up locally now: importer stores exclusive max bounds, debug overlay displays raw object extents, and movement tests include a northeast corner case.

From the tooling side: yes please. If we keep the first two segments stable, I can make the prefab browser group by Area and Category without teaching it seventeen local naming dialects. The CI is my garden and this is weeding.
Animation side looks fine after patch. Slide along counter reads clean. No foot jitter.

Manual check on Marcus’s patch: original repro no longer clips. I can still press diagonally into the corner forever, because QA is a glamorous profession, and the player remains in legal floor space.

Holding final stamp until regression test lands in CI.
Updated my notes with Priya's optional-state version. I will start with the prefabs that are not touched in open PRs, then post a short rename list here before each batch so nobody gets surprised.

title: Block out the game room quadrant
description: Block out the game room with arcade cabinets, the couch zone, and enough clearance for players to drift in and out without snagging on furniture.

title: How we'll collect player feedback (Discord intake)
content:
Playtest is coming up fast, so I want our Discord feedback pipe to be boring in the best way: easy for players, easy for us, and not a 300-message scroll archaeology project by Tuesday night. {{emoji:playtest}}

Proposed setup:

  • #playtest-help for blockers, install issues, crashes, login weirdness, anything that stops someone from getting into Common Room.
  • #playtest-feedback for general impressions, comfort/coziness notes, social flow, "I tried to do X and expected Y" posts.
  • #playtest-bugs for bugs with a pinned mini-template: what happened, where you were, what you clicked, screenshot/video if they have it.
  • #playtest-clips for moments we can learn from, not just quote-tweet fuel. Awkward silence in the game room counts. Someone finding the library nook and immediately sitting down counts more.

Tags on our side in Gameplan: bug, confusing, delight, social-friction, accessibility, performance, content-wish. I’ll do the first pass twice a day, pull patterns into this space as threads, and tag the relevant owner only when there’s an actual question or decision needed.

For tiny Discord anecdotes: yesterday someone asked if the cafe chairs are "real seating or decorative emotional support chairs," which is exactly the kind of sentence I want us to preserve without making designers live in Discord. 🙂

This is the right level of structured.

  • I like twice-daily synthesis instead of live forwarding.
  • Please make one daily "top 5 patterns" post in Player Feedback during the playtest window.
  • Unblocking question: do we want players to self-tag posts, or should tags only happen after your pass?

Tiny marketing caveat: for #playtest-clips, can we ask permission in the pinned note before anyone uploads something they think we might share later? I am absolutely going to ask "but what's the trailer moment?" and I would prefer not to discover the answer inside a consent spreadsheet at midnight.

title: game room blockout (gif)
content: first pass. loop from lobby edge to cabinets to couch.

Repro steps for a healthy feedback pipeline:

  1. Player posts in Discord.
  2. Chloe triages into pattern, bug, or not-actionable-but-useful-human-signal.
  3. Gameplan thread gets created only when the team needs to see it.
  4. QA gets linked to original Discord post for media and timestamp.

Expected result: I do not debug "vibes broken near couch" without knowing which couch. Approved.

This is already doing the thing I wanted the game room to do: it makes me want to drift through it instead of solve it. The cabinet bank reads as a little noise wall, the couch gives you a pause spot without becoming a dead end, and the diagonal-ish path through the middle has enough friction that two players crossing paths will naturally wobble around each other.

Strong opinion, loosely held: keep the main walk lane slightly annoying. Not blocked, just imperfect. If it becomes too clean it turns into a hotel corridor with arcade wallpaper. Right now it feels like a room people rearranged by living in it, which is exactly the flavor.

Also calling it now: this quadrant is going to eat the playtest.

Yes: players will use channel names only, no self-tags. I’ll tag during triage so we don’t accidentally train everyone into filing their feelings like Jira tickets. I’ll add the permission line to #playtest-clips and start the daily top 5 post once playtest opens.

The couch mass is right. Please nobody straighten it. I want the silhouette to stay a little lumpy and social, like someone pulled furniture around to watch a tiny tournament.

Only art-direction caution: the arcade side can get visually loud fast once the cabinet faces exist. I would rather win with chunky silhouettes and two or three strong screen colors than make a rainbow wall. The blockout has a very good rhythm right now: busy edge, soft middle, little pocket at the bottom.

I can wire this for the next room-walk build pretty quickly! The cabinet row gives me nice anchor points for interaction bubbles, and the couch pocket is basically begging for a tiny idle cluster test. If the playtesters do not immediately migrate here, I will eat a variable name.

It is 2:07am and I have become one with tile math! Regression test is in:

it("blocks northeast movement into cafe counter", () => {
  const result = movePlayer({ x: 128, y: 176 }, { x: 1, y: -1 })
  expect(result.position).toEqual({ x: 128, y: 176 })
})

Also added a fixture that compares object-layer collider width against rendered tile width for the cafe map.

CI passed. I also made the collision overlay fail loudly if object bounds are rendered with snapped display coords but logged with raw coords. The tool now tells the same lie in only one place, which is called progress.

Verified fixed in 0.18.4-rc.3.

  • Original northeast cafe-counter repro: pass.
  • Slow nudge, controller, click-to-move: pass.
  • Adjacent wall and counter corners: pass.
  • Library shelf spot check: pass.
  • Regression test visible in CI: pass.

QA approval stamped. The counter is once again a counter.

title: Document our remote rituals

title: Remote rituals: how do we stay a studio, not a Slack?
content:
We have gotten pretty good at making decisions async. I am proud of that. It means the Tokyo morning can move work forward without waiting for San Francisco to wake up, and nobody has to pretend that “everyone online” is a normal state of nature.

But I want to check in on the softer layer: the rituals that make Moonhollow feel like a studio, not just a very organized collection of threads.

What is working for you right now? What feels missing? Small answers are welcome. A weekly screenshot. A song in the playlist. A tiny “I am alive and drinking water” standup. A show-and-tell where we celebrate unfinished things before they become polished.

My bias: rituals should be opt-in, low ceremony, and kind to timezones. They should give people more context and more warmth, not another meeting to survive.

What is working for me:

  • Async standups when they include one “thing I am deciding today,” not just status.
  • Friday show-and-tell, especially when rough work is allowed to stay rough.
  • The shared playlist, because it gives the studio a pulse without needing everyone in the same room.

One unblocking question: do we want rituals to live in one predictable People Ops thread each week, or stay distributed in the spaces where the work already happens?

I vote for distributed, with a tiny weekly “what happened around the clubhouse” index from People Ops. Players do this naturally in Discord: they wander into the cafe channel, drop one screenshot, then someone in another timezone wakes up and adds context six hours later. It feels alive because it is not forced into one hallway. 🌙

Also yes to Friday show-and-tell staying messy. The community team learns so much from seeing the almost-there version before it becomes trailer-shaped.

I like the idea that rituals should create a visible trace. A call vanishes unless someone writes it down. A thread becomes a little studio artifact.

For Common Room, we keep talking about spaces accumulating memory. Maybe our internal version is a weekly “room note”: one sentence from each discipline about what changed in the clubhouse that week. Design moved a chair. Audio found a kettle hiss. Narrative named a shelf. Engineering made the chair stop eating players. It is practical, but it also becomes lore about how the game came together.

Question I maybe should know already: for async standup, is it helpful if interns post “I am confused about X” as the thing we are deciding today? Sometimes that is more true than “working on Y,” but I do not want to turn standup into a support queue.

yes, please post the confusion. a quiet unknown is heavier than a visible one.

for rituals: i would love a shared “listening window” once a week where people can add one track, one field recording, or one sound they found while walking. no meeting, no obligation to explain. just a small shelf of what the week sounded like.

Adopting two things from this thread for next week:

  • Friday show-and-tell stays opt-in and explicitly rough-work-friendly.
  • I will start a weekly “room notes” thread in People Ops that links out to the distributed bits, so the ritual has a home without pulling everything into one place.

And yes, @Felix Novak, “I am confused about X” absolutely belongs in async standup. That is often the most useful line in the whole thread.

title: Early positioning: what's our hook?
content:
I want to start the messy version of this before we get too precious with Next Fest copy.

What is the one-line hook for Common Room, and what is the trailer moment that proves it in three seconds?

Right now I have a few almost-lines:

  • A cozy multiplayer clubhouse where your friends can actually linger.

  • Decorate, gather, play small things, leave notes, come back later.

  • The online hangout that feels like a shared room, not another feed.

None of these are bad, but none make me reach for the wishlist button yet. The trailer moment in my head is four players drifting into different corners of the same clubhouse: someone setting mugs in the cafe, someone paging through the library nook, two people at the tiny game table, then everyone converging in the lobby because somebody dropped a ridiculous chair in the wrong place.

But maybe that is too inside-baseball. What do we think players will repeat to a friend after watching 20 seconds?

From Discord and playtest notes, the player-language keeps clustering around "place", not "activity". People say "I want somewhere to meet up after work" and "this feels like a tiny home base for our group" way more than they say "I want minigames" or "I want customization systems." 🌙

My favorite quote from last week was: "It's like a friend group's living room, but nobody has to clean it." Obviously not store-page copy, but the feeling is very clear.

title: cafe ambience, first loop (headphones on)
content:
first cafe bed is here: cafe_ambience_loop_v01.wav

it is mostly room tone, cup porcelain, a slow kettle, a few soft chair shifts, and the espresso machine staying polite in the next room. no melody yet. just the room remembering that people have been in it all afternoon.

headphones help. speakers are fine if the room is quiet.

one thought i am only half-standing behind: a very low global vinyl-crackle layer under the whole clubhouse, almost felt more than heard. it might glue the spaces together. it might also make the entire game sound like someone found it in a thrift bin, so: gently floating it.

I want to be careful with "your friends can actually linger" because it is emotionally right but mechanically risky. We do not have enough synchronous depth yet to imply this replaces a Friday night call, and I don't want the trailer to sell a fantasy where eight people are constantly chatting, laughing, and doing coordinated stuff.

The thing we can honestly own is softer: the room persists, the traces of people persist, and the game makes it pleasant to overlap briefly. Someone leaves a note by the library shelf. Someone changes the cafe layout. Someone else appears for ten minutes and plays a round at the table. The magic is not "everyone online together." It is "the place remembers that your friends were here."

So my pushback is: if the trailer moment is convergence, make sure it starts with asynchronous traces. Otherwise we accidentally market the least reliable version of remote life.
Loop point is invisible. Chair scrape at 0:41 is excellent.

Oh this is immediately readable as “safe place to idle while your friend is AFK getting tea” 🫖 Discord folks keep asking if the cafe can feel alive without being busy, and this feels like exactly that.

Vinyl crackle: maybe? I like it here, but I can imagine players with sensory stuff wanting a clean version if it is everywhere.

Yes, this is the useful bruise. "The place remembers that your friends were here" is closer to the thing than any of my neat little store-page sentences.

Maybe the trailer moment is not everyone entering at once. Maybe it is: player arrives alone, sees the cafe has changed, finds a note, laughs, moves one chair, and then one friend pops in for a tiny game. The promise becomes presence without scheduling, which feels much more ours.

The loop makes the cafe tiles feel warmer without asking the art to become busier, which I love.

For the crackle, I am a gentle maybe. Tiny localized texture in the cafe feels right. Global crackle risks putting a nostalgia filter over rooms that are supposed to feel present-tense, especially the library nook and conference hall.

Unblocking question for the Next Fest pass: are we aiming the first 20 seconds at "I want this for my friend group" or "I want to live in this room"?

  • If friend group, we need traces, notes, quick overlap, maybe the conference hall used in a non-meeting way.

  • If room, we need stronger cafe/library/game room identity and the pixel art has to do more emotional work up front.

Both are true, but I suspect only one can be the opening hook.

I can wire this up as a per-zone ambience bed with a shared mixer bus pretty easily! If we want to test crackle without marrying it, I can make it a hidden debug toggle plus a slider so people can try “none / barely there / Sam has been left unsupervised.”

thank you, all. i will keep the cafe loop as-is for now and make the crackle a thing we can audition, not a blanket we throw over the whole house while everyone is sleeping.

also yes, the chair at 0:41 is staying. that chair has a tiny life.

title: Level Editor quickstart
content:
This is the fast path for getting a Common Room room from blank grid to playable build without angering the editor, the art folder, or me. The level editor is built around our 16x16 tile grid, four clubhouse quadrants, and the idea that a room should be easy to rearrange ten minutes before playtest because someone discovered the library nook feels like a dentist waiting area.

Start with the right room file

Open the editor from the tools launcher and pick the room you actually mean to touch: central lobby, cafe, library nook, game room, or conference hall. The editor will happily let you make a beautiful change in a copy of the wrong room. It has no moral compass.

  • Lobby is our routing space. Keep sightlines clean and exits readable.
  • Cafe can be dense, but chairs still need clean walk targets.
  • Library nook wants quiet clusters, not a maze of bookcases.
  • Game room supports messier silhouettes, especially around cabinets and couches.
  • Conference hall needs flexible open floor for events and playtest staging.

Tile palette basics

The tile palette is grouped by material first, then trim, then detail. Floor tiles should do most of the work. Detail tiles are seasoning, not soup. If every tile is special, players stop being able to read the room.

  1. Select a base floor tile and block in the room shape before placing props.
  2. Use wall tiles to establish boundaries, then add trim only where it clarifies corners or edges.
  3. Reserve high-contrast detail tiles for interaction zones, entrances, counters, and cozy clutter piles.
  4. Use the checker preview if you are testing seams between tilesets. It is ugly on purpose and useful in spite of that.

If a floor pattern looks cute at 400 percent zoom and noisy at game zoom, it is noisy. The player lives at game zoom.

Prefab placement

Prefabs are the approved way to place repeated objects: cafe tables, reading chairs, arcade cabinets, notice boards, lamps, plant clusters, and the conference snack table that mysteriously survives every redesign. Drag from the prefab tray onto the grid. The ghost preview shows the footprint and the anchor tile.

Use rotation sparingly. Pixel art rotations are not free; some prefabs have hand-authored directions and some have cursed temporary rotations that were useful exactly once. If a rotated object looks like it is leaning away from reality, check whether the prefab has north, south, east, and west variants instead.

Prefab rules that save playtests

  • Leave at least two walkable tiles around social clusters when possible. One tile corridors feel like furniture is judging you.
  • Do not place chairs directly against transition doors unless you want players to spawn into upholstery.
  • Keep interactive props on predictable edges: front face for counters, lower edge for tables, center front for arcade machines.
  • When building a cozy pile, place the functional object first, then decorate around it.

Collision layers

There are three collision layers you will touch in normal editing: solid, soft block, and interaction. Solid means the avatar cannot enter the tile. Soft block means routing should avoid it but can recover through it during path correction. Interaction marks where the player can trigger a thing.

Most furniture gets solid collision on its footprint and interaction on the tile directly in front of the usable face. Rugs, floor decals, shadows, and crumbs from fictional pastries get no collision. Yes, the crumbs are load-bearing emotionally. No, they are not load-bearing physically.

Table prefab example: footprint = 2x2 solid interaction = south edge center clearance = 1 tile minimum on two sides

After editing collision, toggle path preview and drag a route across the room diagonally. Diagonal routes reveal weird single-tile snags faster than walking around politely. Pay special attention to the cafe counter corner, the library shelves near the east wall, and the conference hall podium, because those have all betrayed us before.

Saving rooms

Use Save Draft while working. Draft saves are local to the room branch and safe to spam. Use Export Room only when the room is ready for review or a playtest build. Export writes the tilemap, prefab list, collision layer, spawn markers, and room metadata.

  1. Run validation before export. Fix red errors. Read yellow warnings instead of developing a personality around ignoring them.
  2. Check all door links from the central lobby into the quadrant you edited.
  3. Enter play mode and walk the first thirty seconds a new player would walk.
  4. Export the room and attach the generated preview image to the Gameplan thread.

Playtest checklist

  • Can two players pass each other near the busiest entrance?
  • Can a player identify the cafe, library nook, game room, and conference hall without reading labels?
  • Do interactable props look reachable from the side the interaction expects?
  • Does the room still feel like Common Room: shared, calm, a little lived-in, never grindy?

For anything that smells like an editor bug, tag me in the Engineering thread with the room file, repro steps, and whether validation caught it. The CI is my garden; please label the weeds.

title: First internal playtest is Thursday, here's how to join
content:
Hi all, small large moment: our first internal Common Room playtest is this Thursday.

This is not a balance pass, not a polish review, and not a secret performance benchmark disguised as a hangout. We are looking for the earliest honest read on whether the clubhouse feels like somewhere you want to be with other people.

Build

  • Build link: common-room/internal/playtest-001
  • Checklist: Internal Playtest Checklist
  • Room: Common Room Lobby, then wander anywhere you can reach.
  • Voice: none required. Async notes in the checklist are preferred.

Time windows

  • San Francisco: Thursday 9:00-11:00 AM and 4:00-6:00 PM
  • New York: Thursday 12:00-2:00 PM and 7:00-9:00 PM
  • London: Thursday 5:00-7:00 PM
  • Berlin: Thursday 6:00-8:00 PM
  • Bengaluru: Thursday 9:30-11:30 PM
  • Tokyo: Friday 1:00-3:00 AM if you are already awake, otherwise Friday 10:00-11:00 AM for a quieter pass

What to pay attention to

  • Does it feel cozy, or just small?
  • Where do you get lost?
  • Do the cafe, library nook, game room, and conference hall feel meaningfully different?
  • When another player walks by, does that feel pleasant, neutral, or distracting?
  • What do you try to do that the game does not understand yet?

One unblocking question for everyone: after ten minutes, would you rather stay in the clubhouse or close the build?

Please drop notes in the checklist page as you go. Screenshots are welcome if they clarify a point, but words are more useful than perfect evidence this round.
This is a real milestone. Please protect the spirit of this pass: show up curious, write down what you actually felt, and do not sand off weirdness before we understand it.

I am going to be mildly annoying in the checklist about transitions between rooms, because I think that is where our thesis either lands or quietly falls over. A cozy hangout still needs orientation. If someone leaves the cafe and spends twelve seconds trying to remember whether the library nook is north or east, that is not charming confusion, it is spatial mush.

Also, please note any moment where you instinctively pause because another player is near you. That tiny social hesitation is either the beginning of magic or the beginning of friction, and I want us to know which one it is.

QA read of this announcement:

  1. Download build.
  2. Enter clubhouse.
  3. Attempt to become emotionally attached to furniture.
  4. Record failures with repro steps.

I will keep a separate pass for crashes and obvious blockers so the checklist does not turn into a spreadsheet wearing a scarf.
