title: Internal Playtest Checklist
content:
This is the checklist I use before, during, and after Common Room playtests. It is written as a bug report because that is the only format the universe has provided that can survive memory, timezone math, and someone saying the cafe felt weird three days later.

1. Pre-test Setup

Build and Room State

  1. Install the exact build listed in the playtest thread. Do not test main unless the thread says main. That is how we summon ghosts.
  2. Start from a fresh player profile, then repeat with a returning profile if the test is about continuity, saved outfits, unlocked furniture, or lobby routing.
  3. Verify the clubhouse loads into the central lobby first. The four quadrants should be reachable without menu travel: cafe northwest, library nook northeast, game room southwest, conference hall southeast.
  4. Walk the outer loop clockwise and counterclockwise. At 16x16 tiles, one wrong collision edge is enough to make the place feel like furniture is judging you.
  5. Open the level editor and confirm the loaded map name, tileset version, and collision overlay. Screenshot if anything is ambiguous.

Session Conditions

  • Run at least one solo pass and one multiplayer pass with three or more players.
  • Include one player on a slower connection if available. If not available, write that down instead of pretending the network is a concept.
  • Ask testers to enter from different timezones when possible. We are not optimizing for everyone being online because that condition does not exist.
  • Confirm voice is off unless the playtest explicitly studies call behavior. Common Room should teach itself through space, text, and small interactions.

2. What To Observe

Cozy vs. Lost

Cozy means players slow down because they want to inspect the room. Lost means they slow down because the room has eaten their objective. These look similar in recordings. Do not trust vibes without a timestamp.

  • Watch where the first pause happens after spawn.
  • Note whether players find the cafe counter before asking what to do.
  • Track whether the library nook reads as quiet space or as dead-end storage.
  • In the game room, observe if players understand which objects are playable and which are decorative.
  • In the conference hall, check if the table scale supports gathering or blocks circulation.

If a player says, I think I am supposed to go here, mark it as uncertainty, not success. Correct guesses are still guesses.

Stall Points

A stall is any moment where the player stops progressing without choosing to rest, chat, decorate, emote, or inspect. Stalls are especially useful in Common Room because we have no combat and no grind to blame. The room did it. Congratulations to the room.

  1. Record the timestamp.
  2. Record the tile position if visible in the debug overlay.
  3. Write the last intentional action before the stall.
  4. Write the next successful action after the stall.
  5. Do not diagnose in the observation line. Save theories for notes.

Pixel and Tile Checks

  • Look for objects that break the 16x16 rhythm, especially rugs, chair backs, plant shadows, and cafe stools.
  • Check if interact prompts appear centered on the object the player thinks they are using.
  • Verify diagonal-looking paths are still readable as cardinal movement spaces.
  • Watch for characters partially hiding behind foreground art in ways that look intentional but are not.

3. Filing Findings

Every finding gets repro steps. Yes, including mood notes. If the player says the lobby feels empty, the repro is how they entered, where they walked, what was visible, and when they said it. Feelings have stack traces if we are patient.

Title: [Area] short visible symptom
Build: playtest build number
Players: solo / 2p / 3p / 4p+
Start state: fresh profile or returning profile
Steps:
1. Spawn in central lobby
2. Walk to [quadrant] using [route]
3. Interact with [object] or pause at [tile]
Expected:
Actual:
Evidence: timestamp, screenshot, clip, or notes
Severity: blocks playtest / confuses player / polish / tiny paper cut

Severity Guide

  • Blocks playtest: crash, disconnect loop, impossible navigation, missing spawn, unusable interaction.
  • Confuses player: unclear affordance, misleading path, objective hidden by layout, social space reads as decoration.
  • Polish: animation pop, odd prompt timing, tile seam, inconsistent sound, awkward camera framing.
  • Tiny paper cut: small enough to ship once, annoying enough to become folklore if ignored.

4. Notes For Test Hosts

Do not lead the player unless the test script says to. Ask neutral questions after the moment has passed: What were you looking for there? works. Were you confused by the cafe sign? contaminates the sample and makes me write a longer bug.

When a tester laughs, write down what happened immediately before it. When they go quiet, write that down too. Common Room depends on tiny social signals. Our job is to catch the ones the build currently supports, the ones it accidentally blocks, and the ones it invents by being a little strange in public.

Final requirement: file findings before the next day in your timezone. Async-first only works if the thread contains the memory instead of requiring a live reenactment with hand gestures no one can see.

title: Playtest debrief: what felt cozy?
content:
Positive-half debrief from Friday playtest.

Observed behavior: players entered Common Room intending to test navigation and instead stayed in the game room for 18-27 minutes at a time. Several groups invented their own tiny rituals around the cafe counter before anyone prompted them.

Repro steps:

  1. Spawn four strangers in central lobby.
  2. Let them find the cafe, library nook, game room, and conference hall with no tour.
  3. Wait approximately six minutes.
  4. Watch someone say, "this feels like logging into a place that kept my seat warm."

Expected: some confusion, polite feedback, at least one person asking where the objectives are.

Actual: game room became the gravitational center, cafe felt lived-in, espresso machine got called out by name twice, and ambience carried more emotional weight than any tooltip.

Putting the warm notes here before we go back to the sharper bug list. Week 3 appears to have produced feelings.

My demo notes match this almost exactly.

  • Game room pulled people in without us explaining it.
  • Cafe seating got used as a waiting area, not just decoration.
  • Players noticed the espresso machine sound before they noticed the tooltip.
  • Two testers said the conference hall felt "ready for something" instead of empty.

Unblocking question for this thread: are we treating the game room as the emotional anchor for the demo, or do we still want the cafe to carry that first impression?

I think the game room is winning because it is the first space where the promise becomes legible without instruction. The cafe says "you can pause here," which is lovely. The library says "you can be quiet here," also lovely. The game room says "you can do nothing productively with other people," and that is the whole thesis in a chair.

Strong opinion, loosely held: let the cafe be the threshold and the game room be the proof. If we try to make every room equally sticky for the demo, we flatten the rhythm. A real clubhouse has the room everyone drifts toward. That is not a balance problem unless the other rooms feel dead, and they did not feel dead today.

Also Dev writing "Week 3 appears to have produced feelings" is the most QA sentence ever typed.

Game room as proof, cafe as threshold feels right to me.

What I noticed in the recordings: players understood the cafe as lived-in because the objects were slightly imperfectly arranged. Cups not centered. Stools not perfectly parallel. The counter has enough readable clutter to imply someone was here before you, but not so much that it turns into noise at 16x16.

I am going to protect that. The temptation after a happy test is to add twenty more charming things. We need maybe three more, chosen carefully, and then restraint.

Discord watch party notes: three different people called the game room "the couch room" even though we do not label it that anywhere, which is extremely useful and extremely cute, sorry.

One tester said the cafe "sounds like my apartment building lobby if everyone was nicer." Another asked if the espresso machine had a name. I am not suggesting we name it. I am only reporting facts to QA. {{emoji:espresso}}

The game room thing made me so happy!!! I watched one clip where a player walked to the board game table, stepped away, came back, and then another player mirrored them like they had invented a tiny handshake. No prompt, no reward, no quest marker. Just vibes and collision boxes behaving for once.

Technical note buried inside the feelings: the new proximity interact delay may be helping. People are not accidentally popping panels open every time they brush past furniture, so the room stays social instead of becoming UI soup.

Question from the intern corner: when players invent names for rooms or objects, do we preserve our internal names and let theirs live socially, or do we ever adopt the player language in UI?

I ask because "couch room" seemed instantly understandable in a way "game room" maybe isn't, but I also see how it could make the room feel smaller than it is.

I would keep our labels plain and let player language attach itself organically. "Game room" is functional signage. "Couch room" is what someone says after they have memories there.

The cafe working makes me particularly glad because it implies history without exposition. You do not need a plaque saying this clubhouse has been loved. You need mugs, a low room tone, and the sense that the next person through the door will know where to put their bag.

tiny idle loops are doing work.

chair turn: good. mug steam: good. arcade screen flicker: almost too loud visually.

I will tune flicker down 20%.

title: Rework the conference hall (playtest confusion)
description:
Playtesters are reading the hall as important but not knowing what to do there.

Repurpose or refocus the space so its function is visible from the doorway and still fits the clubhouse tone.

content:
the espresso machine being noticed before the tooltip is the nicest possible bug report.

i think the cafe landed because the loop has little pockets of breath in it. hiss, cup, room, soft chair scrape, then nothing for a second. the nothing is important. it gives people permission to sit there without feeling like the game is asking them to hurry.

i have a cleaner version of the cafe bed with less high-end fizz. posting after i bounce it.

title: playtest snack spreads, post 'em 🍿
content:
Playtest day snack roll call because the Discord folks keep asking what we eat while we lovingly break our own furniture placement rules.

My desk currently looks like a tiny concession stand: popcorn, tea, and what can only be described as a suspicious amount of chocolate. Please add your spreads, crumbs, beverages, heroic leftovers, etc.

Shipping instinct: keep the winning shape, make small fixes, do not chase every compliment into a feature.

demo_anchor = "game_room"
first_impression = "cafe"
change_budget = "tiny"

Also +1 to Marcus on proximity interact delay. The absence of accidental panels is probably why people stayed in conversation mode.

Filed under load-bearing snacks: tortilla chips, salsa, one apple for moral credibility, and coffee that went cold while I was trying to see if two players could both sit on the cafe corner stool at the same time!!!
I had instant ramen, cucumber slices, and three gummy bears I found in my backpack. Is that a spread or evidence?
Question from the player-facing department: if we add a clubhouse snack table someday, does it need the exact same chaotic energy as this thread? I am seeing a lot of tiny bowls doing big work.

@Felix Novak I love the question. My bias: UI uses names that help you navigate; players create names that help them belong. We should not steal every player phrase, but we should listen when their phrase reveals a clearer affordance.

"Couch room" tells us the soft seating is communicating harder than the games. That is fine for now. It might even be why it feels cozy instead of arcade-y.

Repro steps:

  1. Acquire masala peanuts.
  2. Begin testing library nook pathing.
  3. Forget peanuts exist for 41 minutes.
  4. Rediscover peanuts during note cleanup.

Expected: snack consumed steadily. Actual: sudden morale spike at step 4.

title: Playtest debrief: the conference hall confuses everyone
content:
The conference hall is our loudest quiet problem from this playtest.

Across six sessions, players found it, walked around the tables, clicked the chairs, opened the emote wheel, and then asked some version of: “Is this for later?” Nobody hated it. That is somehow worse. It reads like a room with a missing feature, not like a calm corner of the clubhouse.

This is the counterpoint to the “do we need bigger shared spaces?” instinct I have been pushing. A large room only works if players understand what kind of social behavior it invites. Cafe says linger. Library nook says sit together quietly. Game room says poke at stuff. Conference hall currently says we booked the wrong hotel ballroom.

I want us to argue this properly before we patch it with props. Options I see:

  • Repurpose it into something with an obvious everyday fantasy.
  • Cut it for demo and reclaim the map budget.
  • Keep it, but give it a strong identity through art, interaction, and lore.

Also: Ingrid asked in week 1 “what does this room know that the others don’t?” and I did the designer thing where I nodded and then made three seating layouts. I am now formally returning to that question.

I still think the room is telling us it wants to be a public memory rather than a meeting room.

“Conference hall” is a workplace phrase, and the rest of Common Room is very intentionally not workplace-shaped. The cafe is not a break room. The library nook is not documentation storage. The game room is not team bonding. If we keep this space, I would like it to become the clubhouse’s old assembly room: bulletin boards from past clubs, half-finished banners, a little stage, mismatched folding chairs, maybe the sense that people used to gather here for talent shows, town votes, rainy-day movie nights.

That gives players a verb without adding a system: wander in, infer history, maybe leave something small behind later. Empty can be lovely. Purposeless is not.

Repro steps for confusion:

  1. Enter central lobby.
  2. See large doorway north-east.
  3. Enter conference hall.
  4. Observe chairs, long table, podium.
  5. Attempt to interact with podium.
  6. Attempt to sit in every chair.
  7. Say “oh, maybe this is where the devs do announcements.”
  8. Leave.

Expected: room communicates player-facing purpose within ~10 seconds.

Actual: room communicates production calendar.

Additional positive defect, filed here because the tracker has no category for "surprisingly humane."

Tester P07 got separated from their group, walked into the library nook, paused for 11 seconds, then typed: "oh this is where i wait without being awkward."

Repro reliability: unknown. Emotional damage to QA lead: moderate.

I am against cutting the square footage before we try one art-direction pass with a different premise.

The current room is visually honest about the wrong thing. Symmetrical table, centered podium, evenly spaced chairs: all of it says agenda, minutes, projector cable. If this becomes Ingrid’s assembly room, the silhouette changes immediately. Small stage off-center. Stacked chairs against one wall. Handmade signs. A trophy cabinet that has seen things. Floor scuffs that imply dancing, not quarterly planning.

I do not want to solve it by sprinkling “cozy” props over a boardroom. That will read as boardroom wearing mittens.

My selfish marketing question: what is the trailer moment in that room?

If it is “players discover the old assembly hall and one of them hops on stage while others sit on folding chairs,” I can use that. It says hangout, history, and tiny social performance in one shot. If it remains “large room where maybe events happen later,” I will keep cutting around it in captures.

Naive question: if the main issue is “I can’t tell what to do here,” do we need an interactable in the room for the demo? Like a guestbook, stage light, or notice board? Or is that too much feature gravity for one room?

Not naive. That is the question.

I think one light interaction is probably right, but it should confirm the room’s identity rather than apologize for it. A guestbook or notice board says “people gather here and leave traces.” A stage light says “perform here,” which is stronger but may accidentally promise a performance system. Podium is cursed; remove podium from my home.

My vote after reading everyone: do not cut yet. Rename and reframe it, strip the corporate geometry, add one trace-based interaction if engineering says it is cheap. If we start debating the exact chair stack for three days, someone please throw the lobby rug at me. {{emoji:rug}}

Decision path I am hearing:

  • Do not cut the room for this demo pass.
  • Stop calling it conference hall in our working docs unless we mean the current bug.
  • Create a rework task for “assembly room” with art, one possible trace interaction, and playtest success criteria.
  • @Ingrid Halvorsen owns the lore proposal; Lena/Jonas can scope what survives into the demo build.

Unblocking question: is the notice board the candidate interaction, or are we leaving that open inside the task?

Leave it open in the task, but my proposal will lead with the notice board because it gives us the cleanest fiction: the clubhouse remembers gatherings through scraps, flyers, old sign-up sheets, and small notes from players.

I will write the assembly-room lore pass with two versions: minimal demo-safe flavor, and the slightly braver version where the room can collect player traces later. No new system promises in the first version.

This is the week where Common Room started behaving like a place instead of a pitch.

Hold the center. Polish lightly. Let players keep finding their own names for things.

title: Game room won, let's make it sing
content:
The playtest notes are not subtle: people found the game room first, stayed there longest, and described it with the least prompting. That is a signal we should respect.

I do not want to turn it into the loud room. The win, to me, is that it feels like the clubhouse finally has a little gravity. You can idle there without feeling like you are waiting for the real game to start. That is Common Room.

Proposal for a focused polish pass this week:

  • Lighting: warmer pockets around the table and cabinets, cooler falloff at the walls so the room feels deeper without becoming cave-y.
  • One more interactive object: something small and social. My vote is a tabletop spinner or score peg board, not a whole minigame.
  • Sound: soft table taps, a cabinet drawer, maybe one low room tone that says “people have been here.” Nothing that loops itself into annoyance.

I spun off the rough task stubs for art lighting, interaction hook, and sound pass. Please push back on scope. I want this to feel like we found the room's pulse and turned it up by 12 percent, not like we installed a theme park.

Yes to warmer pockets, no to “arcade glow.” I want the game room to feel handled and a little worn in, like the objects have edges from use. I can do a quick light paintover with two options: one more amber around the table, one with the cabinet as the anchor.

Tabletop spinner is extremely doable! I can make it a shared state object with a short cooldown, broadcast the final resting angle, and let animation fake most of the richness. Also gives us a tiny test case for “object everyone can poke without owning it,” which we will need again.

Unblocking question: do we want this polish pass in the next demo branch, or is it okay if the interaction lands after the lighting and sound? My producer heart would prefer:

  • lighting first, because it improves every screenshot
  • sound second, because it changes the feel fast
  • spinner last, because it has the most QA surface

title: Polish pass on the game room (playtest winner)
description:
The game room landed best in playtest, so give it the extra care it earned.

Add a lighting pass, one more small interactive object, and a few sound touches that make hanging out there feel alive.

title: First environmental lore pass
description:
Seed small objects and visual details that imply the clubhouse had a life before the player arrived.

Think noticeboard scraps, mismatched mugs, old event posters, and shelves that tell tiny stories without becoming homework.

title: Environmental storytelling: giving the rooms a memory
content:
I want to propose a light environmental lore layer for the clubhouse: not quest text, not collectible exposition, not plaques on every wall, just small, readable details that make each room feel like it had a life before the player arrived.

Right now the cafe, library nook, game room, and especially the conference hall can read as useful layouts more than remembered places. That is fine for navigation, but Common Room is asking players to believe this clubhouse is shared, old enough to have habits, and generous enough to hold many kinds of social energy. We can help that belief along with objects that imply use: a mug with three paintbrushes in it beside the notice board; a library stamp card tucked under a cushion; mismatched chair labels in the conference hall from a past community vote; a repaired checkerboard with one newer square.

The goal would be memory without homework. A player should be able to walk through and feel, “people have been here,” even if they never articulate why. For those who look closer, the rooms can suggest tiny histories: clubs that formed and dissolved, events that became traditions, someone’s careful fix after something broke.

I am especially interested in the conference hall because it is currently the most at risk of feeling like a multiplayer waiting room with better lighting. It could instead feel like the clubhouse’s public diary: stacked folding chairs from past gatherings, a faded schedule board, a corner where someone always leaves extra pencils, maybe one banner that has clearly been reused too many times. Nothing loud. Just enough evidence.

If this lands, I’d like to make a page with room-by-room object prompts and then turn it into a small lore-pass task for art/design/audio to pull from when we dress the rooms.

Yes to this, and I think the constraint that keeps it from becoming museum glass is exactly what you named: memory without homework.

My design brain wants the layer to do three things at once. First, it should make navigation warmer by giving each room sharper identity. Second, it should invite speculation without requiring a canonical answer. Third, it should give players permission to add themselves mentally to the place. If the conference hall has a reused banner and a stack of chairs with scuffed feet, the player can imagine tonight’s gathering being one more entry in that chain.

The trap is making every object a “lore object.” I’d rather have 8 excellent, quiet details than 40 winks at a backstory. If we can make the conference hall feel like a room that has hosted awkward first meetings, excellent presentations, and someone’s birthday that ran long, I am extremely in.
Spinner idle can settle in 18 frames. Hand nudge can be 6. No bounce unless we want toy energy.

I like this as a scoped pass, especially because it gives the demo rooms more specificity without adding tutorial burden.

  • Can we define “readable at 16x16” as an explicit filter?
  • Can the first pass cover only the four named rooms plus central lobby?
  • Unblocking question: do we want these details to be purely visual for demo, or should a few have hover / inspect text later?

If the answer is “visual now, inspect later maybe,” I can turn this into a lore-pass task and link it to a page once Ingrid has the prompts.

Maya's ordering feels right. Lighting and sound go into the demo branch; spinner can follow if it is clean by Thursday, otherwise it waits and nobody is a villain. Lena, I trust the “not arcade glow” line completely. Marcus and Kenji, please keep the spinner boring in the best way: satisfying once, invisible the tenth time.

From art direction: yes, with a firm hand on silhouette and clutter. The rooms cannot become flea markets.

For the conference hall, I’d steer away from tiny readable writing and toward shapes players understand instantly: stacked chairs, taped floor marks, a storage cart, a fabric banner with obvious age but no legible slogan. The memory should sit in wear, placement, and reuse.

I can make a small visual reference board once the page exists. My line is that every detail must still support the room’s primary read from gameplay distance.

Player-brain report: Discord already invents histories for rooms from basically nothing, so giving them better sparks is going to pay off. Someone called the library nook “where the shy club meets” after seeing one screenshot, which is exactly the kind of gentle projection this supports. 🥲

Also +1 to no homework. The magic is people feeling clever for noticing, not feeling behind because they missed chapter three of chair lore.

title: what the playtest taught me about the mix
content:
i spent the morning with the playtest notes and the capture from saturday, mostly listening for when the room stopped feeling like a room.

the good news: the base ambience is doing more work than i expected. people lingered in the library nook without calling it out, which is usually the sign. the cafe bed also seems to be helping the central lobby feel inhabited without turning into menu music.

the less-good news: the game room is too forward, especially once two table interactions overlap, and the conference hall air loop has a low shelf that makes headphones feel a little boxed in. i also heard the lobby fountain masking footsteps more than it should.

i am going to rebalance those today. also, yes, i am re-floating the very small vinyl-crackle layer for the cafe/library edge. not as nostalgia sauce, just as a soft glue between loops. in the playtest, the quiet zones needed one more human imperfection when players were idle. i have evidence now, which is dangerous for everyone. {{emoji:vinyl}}

title: the door-close animation is in!! (my first shipped thing)
content:
I shipped the door-close animation to main. It is extremely small and also I have been staring at it for three days like it is a feature film.

Door now swings shut, waits for the latch frame, and does the tiny dust puff on contact. I also fixed the thing where it could replay if two people crossed the trigger at the same time.

Thank you to everyone who answered my very detailed questions about sprite anchors, state machines, and why my first version made the door close from the middle like a haunted elevator. I learned a ton.

YES to the quiet zones needing a little human texture 🥺 In Discord, two testers independently said the library felt “peaceful but not empty,” which feels like exactly the lane we want.

Also one person said the game room made them turn their volume down and then they forgot to turn it back up before going to the cafe, so your read matches the player side.
FELIX!!! This door has more emotional range than half the NPCs in my prototype folder. The little puff lands exactly where my brain wanted it to land.

Clean ship. The guard on duplicate trigger events is exactly the kind of boring code that keeps animation feeling magical.

if (door.isClosing || door.isClosed) return

That line deserves a tiny plaque.
CI passed on the first run. I am choosing to interpret this as the door respecting the garden.

Good timing. Puff is late by one frame in the right way.

The hinge reads. Weight reads. Nice work, Felix.

Kenji said the hinge reads, so I am going to print this comment and tape it to my monitor. Possibly to my actual door.

Also I finally understand why we keep the visual state separate from the interaction state. I was fighting the engine until I stopped trying to make the sprite be the source of truth.
game room peaks line up with token bounce anims. not blaming the bounce. just suspicious.

QA report:

  1. Open clubhouse door from lobby.
  2. Walk through with two clients.
  3. Observe door closing.
  4. Feel unexpectedly proud of intern.

Expected: dust puff. Actual: dust puff. Severity: emotional.

i can support this with tiny room memories in the beds of the ambience, not foreground sounds. a soft chair scrape very rarely in the conference hall. distant cafe cup sounds that never become a loop you can count. library fabric and paper more than “shhh.”

no ghosts, just evidence.

Added this to tomorrow's demo checklist under "things to notice on purpose."

  • Door closes after second player exits.
  • Dust puff is visible against cafe floor and lobby floor.
  • No sound yet, just noting the gap for later.

Felix, this is such a lovely first shipped thing.
I know we are not posting this externally yet, but please know that Discord will lose their entire minds over a door that politely shuts itself. The cozy-game people have very refined door opinions.

that tracks. the bounce transients and the little wooden clack are probably stacking in the same tiny pocket. i can duck the room bed under interaction clusters instead of shaving all the charm off the objects.

for the crackle layer, i will make it optically boring: narrow, quiet, no obvious pops, only in cafe/library threshold spots. if anyone notices it as “vinyl,” i have failed.

Answering Maya: visual now, inspect text later maybe. I’ll write the page with that split clearly marked so we do not accidentally design a second interaction system while trying to place a sad little pencil cup.

I’ll keep the first pass to central lobby, cafe, library nook, game room, and conference hall. Each room gets: emotional read, 5-8 object prompts, readability notes, and any audio hooks for @Sam Ellison. Maya, feel free to make the lore-pass task and point it at the page once it exists.

This is small in scope and large in meaning. A room feels shared when it responds after you leave, not only while you are acting on it. The door closing is the clubhouse saying "I saw that, I will take care of the room now."

Also: the dust puff is funny without begging for attention. That is a very hard line to hit. We should celebrate this out of all proportion, and I see we have already begun.

The puff stays inside our texture language: two pixels of lift, no cartoon smoke cloud, no outline noise. Thank you for keeping it modest.

I also like that the door returns the room to quiet. It feels tidy in a way players may not consciously name.

First shipped feature matters. This one also makes the whole game feel more cared for.

Well done, Felix.

title: Environmental Lore Bible (v0)
content:
This is the working bible for how Common Room tells story through rooms, objects, wear, repairs, and tiny suspicious arrangements of mugs. The clubhouse should feel older than the player, cared for by many hands, and currently being re-invented by whoever logs in next. We are not writing lore plaques. We are placing evidence.

Clubhouse Premise

The clubhouse began as a neighborhood public building: part reading room, part meeting hall, part canteen, part after-school refuge. At some point it closed, sat quiet, and collected dust. Now the players have keys and permission, though not much budget. That gives us the emotional spine: nothing is pristine, but everything can be made welcoming.

Every room should suggest three times at once: what it used to be, what the current residents are trying to make it, and what happened there five minutes ago. In 16x16 pixel tiles, that means we lean on silhouettes, repetition, color breaks, and object clusters rather than detail density.

If a player stops for two seconds and says, huh, someone was here, the object did its job.

Quadrants

Central Lobby

The lobby is the shared thesis statement. It is circulation, noticeboard, lost-and-found, and emotional weather report. Story hooks here should change most often because everyone passes through it.

  • Implied history: old visitor desk, patched floor where a heavy sign used to stand, community notices layered over older tape marks.

  • Current life: shoes tucked under benches, umbrella stand with one tiny sword-shaped umbrella, rotating welcome mats from player events.

  • Do not: make it grand. This is not a hotel atrium. It is the place where someone forgot a library book and three people added sticky notes.

Cafe

The cafe should feel practical before it feels cute. Someone has to restock beans, wipe counters, and label the oat milk. Warmth comes from use: chipped mugs, handwritten prep lists, a stool permanently claimed by the shortest regular.

  • Keep food props readable from the top-down camera: tray, steam, cup, jar, crumb line.

  • Let clutter form paths. A player should understand where staff move without us drawing arrows.

  • One mystery per scene is enough: a sealed tin on the top shelf, a receipt for 83 lemons, a mug with a name nobody on the team recognizes.

Library Nook

The library nook is quiet, but not sacred. Books migrate. Cushions get stolen. Someone keeps hiding tiny paper moons between pages. This room carries the most explicit memory of the old clubhouse, so let older objects stay slightly heavier in palette and shape.

  1. Use stacked books to imply ongoing conversations between players.

  2. Reserve rare props, like the locked cabinet or archive cart, for quest and event beats.

  3. Allow mess, but make it intentional. Cozy is not random scatter.

Game Room

The game room is our sanctioned chaos. It can hold mismatched chairs, tangled controller cables, folded card tables, and scoreboards that are mostly lies. It should invite parallel play: one person tinkering with a puzzle machine while two others emote around a board game.

The lore here should be competitive but gentle. Old trophy bases with missing nameplates, a wall bracket shaped for a dartboard that is no longer allowed, a cabinet labeled batteries that contains no batteries. We can be funny without making the room a joke.

Conference Hall

The conference hall is the largest space and the most transformable. Historically it hosted town meetings, recitals, lectures, and possibly one very intense fern club. In current play, it becomes movie night, dev show-and-tell, seasonal markets, and player-built assemblies through the level editor.

Because this room changes often, anchor it with permanent scars: darker tiles where chairs were stacked for years, a crooked stage curtain, wall hooks with no banners attached. Temporary decorations should snap cleanly to the 16x16 grid and avoid covering those memory marks unless the event specifically calls for it.

Object-Level Rules

  • Readable first, clever second. A prop has to function at 1x zoom before it earns a backstory.

  • Prefer relationships over singles. One teacup is decoration. Two teacups, one full and one cold, is a scene.

  • Use wear like handwriting. Scratches, patched fabric, sun-faded rectangles, and uneven stacks should imply habits.

  • Leave room for players. If every surface already tells our story, there is no space for theirs.

  • Avoid fake archaeology. No ancient runes, prophecy walls, or lore dumps pretending to be furniture. Common Room is mundane on purpose.

Level Editor Notes

Player placement will always create stories we did not plan, so our kit pieces need to be generous. Build props in families: clean, worn, repaired, event-dressed. Give each room at least one small set that can cross quadrants without breaking tone, like mugs, folding chairs, floor cushions, notice slips, and plants with questionable survival odds.

Good cluster: chair + half-empty cup + open book + lamp facing wrong way
Bad cluster: chair + chair + chair + chair + lore-sign

Playtest Watchlist

During playtests, please note where people pause, point, laugh, or invent explanations. Also note where they miss a story beat entirely. The best environmental lore in Common Room should survive without a tooltip, but if nobody reads the scene, the scene is not being polite enough.

Open questions for the next pass: how many persistent object states can we afford per room, whether seasonal overlays should age the clubhouse or simply dress it, and which props players can rename without turning the lobby into a fridge covered in ransom notes.

title: rain on a cafe window, recorded this morning
content:
walked to the corner cafe before most of the street was awake and got 11 minutes of rain ticking against the front window. no music, just glass, cups, one chair scrape, and the espresso machine sighing once like it had opinions.

not sure if it belongs in common room or if it is just saturday brain, but it made the cafe nook feel immediately more lived-in to me.

leaving it here for the weekend hush.

title: Write the studio onboarding guide
description: Write the practical guide for how Moonhollow works: async-first decisions, Gameplan threads, timezone manners, and the few rituals we actually keep. Include the tools new folks need to ship on Common Room without needing three welcome calls.

title: Moonhollow Onboarding Guide
content:
Welcome to Moonhollow. We make Common Room, a cozy top-down multiplayer hangout game about shared spaces, small rituals, and the joy of moving a 16x16 chair one tile to the left until it finally feels right. This guide is the practical version of how we work together: async-first, kind, specific, and respectful of the fact that San Francisco and Tokyo are rarely awake at the same time.

How We Work

Gameplan is our office. If something matters, it should live in a thread, page, task, or decision note here. Calls are useful for bonding, playtests, gnarly debugging, and the occasional shared laugh at a door animation that somehow opens into a bookshelf. They are not where decisions disappear.

  • Default to async. Write the context, the tradeoff, and the decision you need. Give people time to answer.
  • Assume someone is asleep. If your note requires an instant reply, check whether it really does. Most things can wait a workday.
  • Disagree in the artifact. Point at the mockup, build, tilemap, branch, or player behavior. We critique the work so the work gets better.
  • Leave breadcrumbs. Future us should be able to understand why the cafe counter is three tiles wide without excavating six chats.

If a decision affects more than one person, put it in Gameplan. If it affects the player, include the player reason.

Timezones and Availability

Our current spread runs roughly from San Francisco to Tokyo. Nobody is expected to be online all the time, and being quiet is not a signal of disengagement. Please keep your working hours in your profile, update PTO early, and use status notes when you are heads-down or away.

We try to maintain a four-hour courtesy window for non-urgent replies during your working day. That is a guideline, not a leash. Deep work beats notification whack-a-mole.

Rituals

Monday Intentions

Every Monday, post your focus for the week in the studio thread: one or two concrete outcomes, any blockers, and anything you need eyes on. Good examples: finish library nook seating pass, test conference hall capacity with 12 players, write copy for cafe bulletin board interactions.

Wednesday Build Notes

By Wednesday, each discipline posts a short build note. Art might share the latest 16x16 tile sheet changes. Engineering might flag level editor regressions. Design might ask whether the game room minigame table needs clearer affordance. Keep it skimmable and link the branch, clip, or page.

Friday Playtest

Friday playtests rotate between friendly chaos and targeted tests. Sometimes we all pile into the central lobby to see where pathing breaks. Sometimes five people quietly test the cafe ordering flow while everyone else watches recordings later. If you cannot attend, add notes async. Recordings and findings go in the Playtests space.

Common Room Context

The clubhouse is organized around a central lobby with four main quadrants: cafe, library nook, game room, and conference hall. When you write feedback, name the place and the player behavior. The cafe feels cramped is less useful than two players entering from the lobby collide near the pastry case because the counter blocks the diagonal path.

Our level editor is powerful and therefore occasionally dramatic. Before making layout changes, check whether the tile is part of a shared set, whether collision is intentional, and whether the prop has interaction copy. When in doubt, ask in the Level Editor thread before reshuffling a whole quadrant.

Decision note pattern:
Context: What are we changing?
Player reason: What gets better for someone in Common Room?
Options: What did we consider?
Decision: What are we doing now?
Revisit: What would make us change our minds?

Tooling

  • Gameplan: decisions, tasks, pages, specs, ritual threads, PTO notes.
  • GitHub: code review, branch discussion, release blockers.
  • Figma: UI flows, prop sheets, icon exploration, handoff comments.
  • Discord: lightweight social chatter and live playtest voice. Do not bury decisions there.
  • Builds folder: playable builds, recordings, and regression clips.

Who To Ask

  • People, PTO, onboarding, rituals: @hana
  • Game direction and feature priority: @mika
  • Pixel art, tiles, prop readability: @bea
  • Level editor and tools: @eli
  • Networking, multiplayer presence, build stability: @rowan
  • Writing, object barks, room tone: @jules
  • Playtest coordination and bug triage: @nina

PTO and Being Human

Please take your PTO. Put it in Gameplan as soon as you know, note anything that needs coverage, and resist the urge to write a tiny novel apologizing for resting. We plan around absence because absence is normal.

If you are sick, tired, moving apartments, caring for family, or just running at half battery, say what you can and step back. You do not owe the studio a performance of being fine. The clubhouse we are building is warm because the people making it have room to be people.

First Week Checklist

  1. Update your profile, timezone, pronouns if you want, and working hours.
  2. Read the current Milestone page and the latest Friday playtest notes.
  3. Install the current build and spend 20 minutes wandering the clubhouse.
  4. Post a short hello in the Welcome thread, including one game you keep returning to.
  5. Pair async with your buddy on one tiny contribution: a bug note, copy pass, tile review, or docs cleanup.

Ask questions in public when you can. Someone else probably had the same question and was waiting for a brave person to type it first.

title: 2am and I'm just watching felix's door close over and over
content:
It is 2:07am and I was allegedly polishing the cafe entry timing, but I keep tabbing back to Felix's new door-close animation because the last 3 frames have unreasonable serotonin in them.

The tiny pause before it settles? Criminal. I have replayed it enough times that the clubhouse is now basically a door simulator with chairs.
This made my whole night. I spent an embarrassing amount of time moving that last pixel down and then back up again, so I am very glad the door has found its audience.
Audience of one, fully converted. I am putting the build down before I start giving the hinge notes.

title: New studio onboarding guide (and a shout to Felix)
content:
I just published a new onboarding guide page for future Moonhollow folks: New studio onboarding guide.

It pulls together the practical things we usually explain across five different threads: how we use Gameplan, what deserves a decision thread, where to ask tiny questions, how PTO works, who owns what, and the very important reminder that nobody is expected to be online at the same time as everyone else.

Also, a quiet shout to @Felix Novak. A lot of this got sharper because Felix kept asking the beginner questions out loud this month, then turned around and shipped pieces of the clubhouse flow with more confidence each week. That is exactly the kind of learning-in-public I want us to make room for.

Please leave fixes or missing bits here. Especially anything that would have helped you in your first week.

This is very usable. The thing I like most is that it explains the studio by showing where work actually happens.

  • The decision thread examples are concrete.
  • The async expectations are stated without sounding apologetic.
  • The PTO bit is blessedly direct.

One unblocking question: should we add a tiny “first Friday” checklist for managers, or does that belong in a separate People Ops page?

Thank you, Hana. Asking the obvious questions felt awkward for maybe two days and then I realized half the team was silently relieved I had done it. The “small questions are work too” line in the guide would have made my first Monday much less mysterious.

Also yes to a first Friday checklist. I would have used that.

title: Early Discord reactions to the playtest clips 👀
content:
I dropped the short cafe + library nook playtest clips into Discord this morning and the first outside-the-studio reactions are very, very encouraging.

Big themes so far: people immediately clocked the shared clubhouse vibe, several called out the pixel lighting as "warm but readable," and one person said they wanted to "log in and just be there after work," which I am choosing to print on an invisible banner above my desk.

Still early, obviously, but this is the first time the cozy promise is landing with people who have not been staring at Common Room every day. Good wind in the sails for Next Fest. {{emoji:cozy}}

This is such useful signal, thank you for bringing the player voice in here.

  • "I want to be there after work" is exactly the emotional target.
  • Readability praise matters because the clips are small and compressed.
  • The cafe/library pairing seems to explain the game faster than our feature list does.

One unblocking question: can we tag the strongest 5-6 Discord quotes for possible Next Fest capsule/trailer use, even if we only keep them internal for now?

My marketing brain is making a tiny siren noise. The hook here is not "multiplayer cozy clubhouse," it is the moment someone sees two players wordlessly settle into the same room and thinks, oh, I know what this is for.

But what's the trailer moment? Is it the cafe clip, the library nook, or the cut where players drift through the lobby and choose different corners?

Adding a tiny bit of color from chat: the clip that got the fastest "wait I love this" pile-on was actually the lobby drift into the cafe, not the static cozy shot. People seemed to respond to the invitation more than the room itself.

title: The playtest clip we should be putting in front of creators
content:
I pulled the 7-second clip from yesterday's playtest where four friends all try to sit on the game room couch, realize it supports the whole pile, then immediately start emoting like they discovered furniture-based democracy.

This is the outreach clip. Not the cafe pour, not the library quiet moment, not the lobby walk-in. Those are lovely, but this sells Common Room in three seconds: friends enter a tiny shared space and instinctively make a bit together.

For creators, I would lead with this in the first email GIF. For press, I would put it above the fold in the Next Fest note. It says cozy multiplayer without asking anyone to read the phrase cozy multiplayer.

Yes, this is the one. The Discord playtest channel was basically live-captioning that couch pile for ten minutes, including someone asking if the couch had union protections.

I can route the clip to the friendly streamer list today with a short note: this is what happens when the room gives players permission to be ridiculous together. Feels much stronger than asking them to imagine the vibe from screenshots.

I like it because it is not a feature demo pretending to be a feeling. It is the feeling, with enough mechanics visible that people understand there is a real toy underneath it: collision, seating, emotes, readable avatars, the game room identity.

If we are using Next Fest to answer one question, I think the question is not "what do you do in Common Room?" It is "why would I want to be there with someone else?" The couch answers that faster than a bullet list ever will.

That frames the Next Fest decision cleanly for me: lead with couch pile, support with cafe/library/conference hall as breadth, and stop trying to make the first beat carry every room in the clubhouse.

title: Bluebird: a game about the room you leave behind
content:
I am putting this here while it is still mostly fog, because fog is the correct material for it.

Working codename: Bluebird. Not a sequel to Common Room. Maybe not even the same emotional weather system. If Common Room is about making a place feel lived-in together, Bluebird might be about the last hour before a place stops being yours.

The player is in one room. Maybe an apartment bedroom, maybe a rented studio, maybe a childhood room after everyone has politely agreed not to say childhood. You are packing, but the verbs are not really packing verbs. You fold, choose, leave, listen, misremember. The room changes because attention changes it.

I do not want to make a sad object collector. I also do not want to make a trauma box with nicer pixels. The thing that interests me is quieter: what if the whole game is the act of deciding what the room gets to keep of you?

  • Small scope: one room, one evening, maybe one visitor who never fully appears.
  • 16x16 discipline still applies, but less bright, more negative space.
  • No fail state. No perfect ending. No checklist completion glow.
  • Sound carries a lot: outside traffic, old pipes, distant party, a bird at the wrong hour.
  • Mechanically, I keep circling verbs like place, cover, open, leave.

This is not a proposal yet. More like: I saw a little blue light under a door and wanted to write down what I think might be behind it.

What if the room is not haunted by a person, but by versions of the player who made different departures?

The bluebird could be literal only once: heard before seen, then understood as the one thing that does not belong to the room. A little impossible witness. It leaves when you finally stop arranging the space for someone else's return.

Cautiously excited by this. I like that it is a counterpoint, not a rebuttal.

My guardrail would be: if we do this, it should stay small enough that the team can hold it gently. One room sounds right. One emotional promise, kept very cleanly, sounds better.

Grounding this before it grows legs in six directions:

  • Can the first playable question be one room, three objects, one irreversible choice?
  • Can we prove the tone without custom systems beyond movement, inspect, place, and leave?
  • Can we treat this as a two-week mood prototype someday, not a shadow roadmap?

I am very into the little blue light under the door. I am also standing in front of the production door with a clipboard.

title: RFC: cleaning up the interaction system before the demo
content:
I want to do a small cleanup pass on the interaction system this week before we lock the demo branch. This is not a rewrite. I am specifically trying to remove the three places where we currently answer “can the player interact with this?” slightly differently.

What I learned shipping Felix's door animation is that the interaction code is mostly fine until timing enters the room. The door worked once the prompt, lock state, animation state, and network event stopped each trying to be the source of truth for the same moment.

Proposal:

  • Keep detection in InteractionSensor: what is nearby, what is focused, what prompt should show.
  • Move eligibility into Interactable.canUse(player, context): locked, occupied, cooldown, host-only, etc.
  • Keep effects in Interactable.use(player, context): open door, sit, start board game, ring cafe bell.
  • Add one tiny debug overlay that shows focused id + reason if blocked, because I would like future us to stop reading tea leaves in the console.

I can put this behind the current API so rooms do not have to migrate all at once. First pass would be lobby doors + library chairs since they cover animation and occupancy without touching the conference hall flow.

If this sounds reasonable, I’ll make the branch today and keep the diff boring.

Yes. This is the right size and the right boundary.

if (!interactable.canUse(player, context)) return showBlockedReason()
await interactable.use(player, context)

Only note: make the blocked reason optional for shipped builds so we do not accidentally localize debug prose at 11:58pm.

title: Which quadrant leads the demo? (poll)
content:
I opened the poll because I think we have to stop pretending all four quadrants can be polished to Next Fest quality in the time we have. We can make the whole clubhouse present, but one quadrant needs to be the thing players remember when they close the demo.

My bias before watching last week's playtest was Cafe: it reads instantly, it explains the fantasy, and it gives us cozy verbs without a tutorial wall. But the Game Room did something I care about more: players started inventing little rituals around it. They waited for each other at the table, swapped seats, watched someone fail at the tile puzzle, then drifted back into chat without being told what the loop was.

So: vote with the demo viewer in mind, not the full game in your heart. Which quadrant should get the most polish pass, interaction density, animation budget, audio attention, and QA time?

title: Deliver final capsule / key art
description:
Finish direction B for the Steam page: the crew gathered in the central lobby with the cafe and library peeking in at the edges.

Prioritize readable silhouettes at small sizes over stuffing in every favorite prop.

title: Refactor the interaction system before the demo
description:
Clean up interaction handling according to Felix's RFC so doors, seats, shelves, and cafe props all go through the same predictable path.

The demo build should not need special cases taped under every counter.

content:
Voting Game Room unless someone sees a production trap I missed.

  • It had the strongest playtest retention.
  • It gives trailer capture more varied motion than Cafe.
  • It lets us polish one social toy deeply instead of spreading tiny interactions everywhere.

Unblocking question: if Game Room wins, are we comfortable moving Cafe from “centerpiece” to “arrival texture” for the demo build?

This would also clean up the game room table weirdness where the prompt says “Join” for one frame after the table fills. I have been pretending not to see it because the bug only appears when I test like a raccoon with a keyboard, but yes please!

Minor ask: can canUse return a stable reason enum instead of a string? I want to hook one or two of those into the playtest telemetry without scraping text.

Repro steps for my vote:

  1. Put three external playtesters in lobby.
  2. Wait 90 seconds.
  3. Observe one person enter Game Room.
  4. Observe the others follow without prompt.
  5. Observe chat becomes about what they are doing, not where the controls are.

Expected result: demo lead quadrant teaches the game. Actual result: Game Room did. Cafe was second, but needed more nudging from us.

I can support Game Room as the lead, with one art-direction condition: it cannot become “the loud quadrant.” The appeal in the footage is that it feels like people found a corner of the clubhouse, not that we built an arcade.

If this wins, I want the polish pass focused on tactile readable objects: worn tabletop edges, chair silhouettes, soft lamp pools, clear board states, and idle animations that do not compete with player movement. Cafe can still do the first-impression warmth from the lobby edge.
Tiny tooling note: if you add the debug overlay behind VITE_INTERACTION_DEBUG, the CI is my garden and I can keep the weeds out of demo builds.

Good calls. I’ll use a reason enum with a debug-only label map, and gate the overlay with VITE_INTERACTION_DEBUG. Starting with doors/chairs still feels like the smallest honest proof, so I’ll open the PR against that slice.

Discord read from the clips: people described the Cafe as “cute” and “I want to live there,” which is good, but Game Room got the “wait what happens if we all sit down?” energy. That is the demo energy imo. Tiny bit of mystery, very low stakes, immediately social. 🎲

title: Bluebird: next prototype build pass
description: Extend the private Bluebird prototype with a larger walkable loop and a few interaction spots that test the feeling, not the roadmap. Keep it clearly separate from Common Room production assets so nobody accidentally pitches it in a screenshot.

title: Cut the Next Fest trailer
description: Cut a 30-45 second Next Fest trailer that leads with the couch-pile moment in the central lobby. Show the cafe, library nook, game room, and conference hall as places players naturally drift between, not as a feature checklist.

title: The trailer moment: what's the ONE shot?
content:
I keep coming back to the same playtest note: people understand Common Room when they see one good social collision happen, not when we explain rooms/features.

So here is the RFC version of my hunch: what single shot sells the game in three seconds?

  • Couch pile: four or five players squeezing onto the game-room couch, someone doing the tiny sit shuffle, cozy chaos. Very clip-friendly. Risk: reads as cute but not necessarily multiplayer.
  • Cafe at golden hour: warm windows, mugs on tables, two players drifting through, one at the counter. Gorgeous. Risk: sells the mood more than the promise.
  • Whole crew entering the lobby: avatars arrive from different room entrances into the central lobby, a wave or two, someone beelines for the cafe, someone pauses by the notice board. Risk: harder to stage cleanly at 16x16, but it says “this is a place we meet.”

I want us to pick the shot that becomes the trailer opener, the Steam capsule thumbnail candidate, and probably the key-art poll seed. My bias right now: lobby entrance, but I am ready to be wrong loudly and efficiently.

From Discord/playtest brain: the couch pile got the most “lol wait can i sit too?” comments, but the lobby entrance got the strongest “ohhh this is everyone’s place” reaction. I’d use couch pile as the gif reply bait, not the first three seconds.

I think the first shot has to communicate the premise, not the texture.

The couch pile is texture. Good texture! It says the game has closeness and comedy and that the sprites have enough physicality to make proximity funny. The cafe is also texture, maybe the cleanest texture we have. It says “warm” immediately, which matters for Next Fest because people are scanning for emotional category.

But the lobby entrance is the premise. People arriving from different directions into one shared center is basically the whole design thesis in a tiny stage play. If we can make the staging readable, it tells the viewer: not farming, not decorating alone, not a chat room wearing pixels. It is a clubhouse where the good part is crossing paths.

Strong opinion, easy to move off it if @Lena Sorensen says the composition turns into sprite soup.

Lobby entrance is very doable if we cheat the timing! We can spawn the “arrival” beats in a 1.8 second cascade so it feels like everyone just got there together without actually having eight sprites overlap in the same doorway. Also I can make the wave emote fire on a marker for the capture build, which is absolutely a normal sentence and not evidence of a cursed profession.

Calling it: Game Room wins. Cafe is close enough that it stays emotionally important, but not close enough to split the polish effort.

Decision for the demo: Game Room becomes the centerpiece. Cafe becomes the warm threshold, Library gets a quieter browse pass, and Conference Hall is officially not demo-load-bearing. The almost-no-votes result there is useful, not sad. It means we stop varnishing something that wants a rework.

I will rewrite the quadrant polish notes around this and tag the relevant tasks. Strong opinions now held slightly less loosely.

I love the cafe shot as an image, but I do not think it should carry the first impression. Golden hour makes everything feel finished and solitary. Beautiful, yes. Slightly too “please admire this room.”

The lobby can work if we treat it like a group portrait in motion: clear silhouette lanes, no one centered exactly on the same axis, and the warmest light hitting the middle without turning the scene orange. The couch pile should be our second laugh, not our handshake.

Unblocking question: are we choosing the shot for emotional clarity or asset reuse?

  • If emotional clarity wins, I hear lobby entrance.
  • If asset reuse wins, cafe is safer because key art, capsule, and trailer frame can share more composition.
  • If “people will post this” wins, couch pile is probably the clip.

My producer vote is lobby entrance, then we deliberately harvest couch pile for socials.

lobby entrance gives me the cleanest sound idea too: little room tones folding into one shared bed, footsteps becoming rhythm, then the soft chime when the last person crosses the center tile. cafe is pretty, couch is funny, lobby has a downbeat.

title: Key art directions, pick one (poll)
content:
I did the responsible thing and made three tiny pictures to answer one enormous question. Please vote in the poll before I convince myself the correct answer is "none, start over, become a baker."

A is the warm cafe hero shot. B is the whole crew gathered in the lobby. C is the cozy night exterior of the clubhouse.

My gut says B has the clearest "this is a place with people in it" read, but I am emotionally compromised by every window light in C.

title: Key art direction for the Next Fest capsule
options: B. The whole crew in the lobby

content:
Okay, I think we have enough gravity here: lobby entrance is the trailer-opener candidate. Couch pile becomes the social cutdown moment, cafe stays in the mood reel / capsule exploration.

I’ll put the key-art poll together around “group entering the lobby” vs two adjacent framings, not three unrelated concepts. Much better question. Thank you for preventing me from A/B/C-ing us into soup.

B is the strongest capsule read for me. The silhouettes make the lobby feel like a stage without turning it into a poster for eight individual heroes, and the warm/cool split from cafe to library gives us enough color travel at thumbnail size.

C is beautiful, but it is more mood than promise. A is useful for trailer beats, less useful as the first Steam shelf impression.

Voted B. It says multiplayer in one glance, which is the thing I keep trying to force into every Next Fest sentence.

Also: it gives us a clean trailer moment if we match cut from the capsule into the actual lobby filling up. Wishlist brain is happy.

I am voting B with one small caveat: let the center of the room stay breathable. The fantasy is "I can join this hangout," not "I need to memorize this cast." If the empty tile or two in the middle survives the finish, it will do a lot of quiet work.

And let's not rug this one. Pick the lobby, protect the read, ship the capsule.

option: B. The whole crew in the lobby

content: B is also the one I can imagine Discord grabbing onto fastest. The moment there are multiple little bodies doing different tiny things, people start naming them and projecting friend groups onto them. That happened with the library nook crop and it had, like, two chairs and a plant.

title: Bluebird prototype, first walkable build
content:
First walkable Bluebird thing is up in the private build folder. It is mostly a room, a doorway, and several questionable rectangles wearing hats, but you can move around and the camera behaves.

Please keep this inside Prototype Builds for now. I am not ready for the wider room to learn how little of this is currently held together by actual game.

Oh.

This is already doing the thing I hoped it might do, which is making the room feel like it has a memory without telling me what happened there. The lit doorway is the whole prototype for me right now. I want to walk toward it, but I also want to inspect the empty floor first, which is a very good problem.

Early instinct: Bluebird should probably be smaller than Common Room in verbs, not bigger. Walk, look, wait, maybe leave something behind. The cool palette buys us a lot of quiet.

The doorway feels less like an exit and more like a promise someone forgot to keep. I mean that as a compliment.

If Common Room is a place where people arrive and soften, Bluebird could be the thing that happens after everyone has gone home: the chairs still warm, the lights not quite off, the room deciding what it remembers. I would resist explaining the bird too soon. Let it be a pressure in the walls for a while.

option: B. The whole crew in the lobby

content:
B is leading by a lot now. Unblocking question: do we need final capsule crop tests before Tomas starts polish, or can those happen in parallel?

  • Steam small capsule
  • library capsule
  • press kit hero crop

I can make the task once Lena confirms the crop order.

Parallel is fine. Use B as the direction. Crop priority should be small capsule first, then library capsule, then press kit hero.

@Tomas Rivera please keep the lobby read and character grouping from the rough. We can lose prop detail before we lose the multiplayer read.

Tiny producer hat, because this is how I show affection:

  • Unblocking question: do you want feedback on feel/mood only, or are input/camera bugs useful at this stage?

Please make that the key-art direction task.

option: B. The whole crew in the lobby

title: Next Fest is a marathon, not a crunch (read this)
content:
Next Fest energy is very real right now. I love seeing everyone care this much about the demo, the tiny details, and the player experience.

Also: Next Fest is not permission to crunch.

  • PTO already approved still stands.
  • Do not quietly trade sleep for polish.
  • If your list is too full, flag it early in your thread or with Maya so we can cut scope or move work.
  • No one should be waiting for replies across impossible timezone windows.
  • If something feels urgent after your day is done, write the handoff and leave it for the next person online.

The demo is important. So are your brains, wrists, families, dinners, walks, and the part of you that remembers games are supposed to be alive. We can be ambitious without pretending exhaustion is a production strategy.

Keep it here for now. Small circle, careful notes, no hallway whispers.

Also: Tomas, that doorway is not a questionable rectangle. It is a very confident rectangle.

Thank you. One practical thing from production: if you are at capacity, please do not wait until the daily demo note to say it. Drop it in the relevant feature thread with one line:

  • what is at risk
  • what you recommend cutting or moving
  • who needs to know

That gives me enough to reshuffle without making you carry the whole puzzle in your head.

option: B. The whole crew in the lobby

content:
Backing Hana explicitly here. We are not going to buy a better Next Fest week with a worse studio afterward.

If the choice is between a smaller demo made by people who are still themselves, and a larger demo made by people running on fumes, the smaller demo wins. Every time.

option: B. The whole crew in the lobby

title: Next Fest demo scope: the checklist
content:
Converting Grace's announcement into the working checklist so we can stop carrying the demo in six different heads. This is producer mode, not stone tablet mode, so push back where the scope is wrong.

Demo must include

  • Multiplayer presence: 8-player room cap, join/leave, names, basic emotes, sit/stand, and no combat verbs leaking into UI.

Cut from Next Fest

  • Clubhouse decoration editing.
  • Library book trading.
  • Private rooms.
  • Seasonal cosmetics.
  • Any account migration work beyond the demo guest flow.

Owners

  • @Priya Sharma: multiplayer stability, guest flow, final feature flags.
  • @Zoe Tran: build pipeline, Steam demo branch, symbols, build notes.
  • @Dev Patel: QA pass plan, repro template, demo acceptance checklist.
  • Jonas: confirm guided moment scripts and what we are willing to leave weird.
  • Antoine: trailer capture beats and store-page wording.

I spun off the plan page as Next Fest Demo Scope and task stubs for the three owner lanes above. One unblocking question: do we treat the conference hall prompt as a real scheduled event, or fake it as a local timed beat for the demo?

title: Steam Next Fest demo plan
content:
This is the working plan for the Common Room Steam Next Fest demo. The goal is not to show every corner of the clubhouse. The goal is to let a new player understand the promise in ten minutes: walk into a warm shared space, find another person, do one tiny activity together, and leave wanting the full room to open up.

Demo Promise

For Next Fest, we are shipping a focused slice of the clubhouse around the central lobby and the game-room quadrant. The cafe, library nook, and conference hall need to feel present as places in the world, but they do not all need full interaction depth in the demo. We are selling the feeling of the whole clubhouse through one polished route, not by thinning ourselves across four half-finished quadrants.

Success looks like: a streamer can invite one friend, customize a little corner, play one table activity, wave at a stranger, and understand why Common Room is a place to return to.

Scope In

  • Central lobby: spawn point, soft onboarding, readable paths to all four quadrants, and the bulletin board with two sample async posts.

  • Game-room quadrant: the featured area. Fully dressed pixel art using the 16x16 tile grid, one playable table activity, couch seating, shelf props, ambient arcade cabinet loops, and clear multiplayer affordances.

  • Light avatar customization: 6 hair options, 6 shirts, 4 skin tones, 4 accessory slots. Enough to feel personal, not a character creator sinkhole.

  • Room reactions: wave, sit, clap, and tiny confetti. These need to be readable at 1x and 2x zoom.

  • Level editor read-only showcase: one curated editor view that shows our tile placement workflow without letting demo players save layouts. This is a feature teaser, not a tool release.

  • Playtest telemetry: join success, time to first interaction, table activity completion, and disconnect reason.

Scope Out

  • No cafe ordering flow. The espresso machine can hiss and look charming, but nobody is pulling shots yet.

  • No library catalog, book exchange, or shelf tagging.

  • No conference hall scheduling. We can show the big room locked for renovation with a sign that does not make us sound like a bank.

  • No player-created rooms, public room browser, trading, quests, daily rewards, grind loops, combat, or economy.

  • No full level editor access in the Steam build. Internal editor builds stay internal until undo, collision validation, and tile palette permissions stop biting us.

Featured Quadrant: Game Room

The game room is our demo anchor because it gives players an obvious reason to gather without making the whole experience feel like a minigame wrapper. Layout target is 24 by 18 tiles, with the lower edge open to the central lobby. Please keep sightlines clean: a new player standing at the lobby threshold should see the table, at least one seat, and one animated prop.

Must-Hit Beats

  1. Player enters from lobby and sees another avatar already doing something legible.

  2. Player sits at the tabletop without needing chat instructions.

  3. Two players complete a short cooperative pattern-matching round in under three minutes.

  4. After completion, the room reacts with a small shared payoff: cabinet lights, confetti, and a sticker on the table ledger.

Demo route: spawn -> lobby board -> game room threshold -> sit -> play -> react -> peek at cafe/library/conference hall

Milestones and Owners

  • July 12, vertical slice locked: Theo owns game-room tiles and collision pass. Lina owns table activity rules. Sam owns network join stability. Maya owns playtest script.

  • July 19, content complete: Priya owns avatar options. Jules owns lobby signposting and copy. Ken owns SFX pass for footsteps, cabinet hum, seat snap, and round win.

  • July 26, external playtest one: 12 players across San Francisco, Berlin, Bangalore, and Tokyo timezones. We are testing confusion, not taste.

  • August 2, demo freeze: bug fixes only after this. New cute prop ideas go into the parking lot, even if they are devastatingly good little lamps.

  • August 9, Steam build candidate: store page capsule, trailer cut, screenshots, controller note, and known-issues thread ready.

Playtest Notes

Because everyone online is never true, every playtest needs an async fallback. The lobby board should have two seeded posts: one inviting players to meet in the game room, and one showing a completed table ledger from an earlier group. If a solo player lands in the demo, they should still understand that this is a shared clubhouse, not an empty room with nice flooring.

During playtests, please write down the exact tile or object where confusion happens. Got lost near library corner is useful. Vibes off is a weather report.

Path to the Fest

Steam Next Fest players will judge us quickly, so the build needs to be boring in the places where boring is good: fast boot, reliable invite link, clean disconnect recovery, readable controls, stable audio mix, and no mystery loading after joining a room. Our weirdness belongs in the clubhouse details, not in setup friction.

Unblocking question: can we commit to the cooperative pattern-matching table as the only playable activity for the demo by July 12?

If yes, we protect polish time. If no, we need to name the alternative this week and cut something else of equal size. My vote is yes: one delightful table beats three nervous prototypes.

Repro steps for accepting this scope:

  1. Install Steam demo build on clean machine.
  2. Join as guest.
  3. Enter lobby, cafe, library nook, game room, conference hall.
  4. Invite seven imaginary coworkers, because apparently that is QA now.
  5. Trigger coffee order, board-game table, and conference hall prompt.
  6. Quit, relaunch, confirm nothing promises features we cut.

I will own the acceptance checklist and bug triage labels. Please do not sneak decoration editing back in under a name like "chair feelings".
