title: Creator & press outreach plan for Next Fest
content:
Putting the Next Fest outreach plan here so it is visible and boring in the useful way. Please treat this as the plan of record unless we find a sharp reason to change it.

Goal

Get Common Room in front of cozy-game players before the demo window opens, then make it easy for creators and press to cover the demo without needing a synchronous briefing from us. The pitch is: a cozy top-down multiplayer clubhouse about presence, little rituals, and shared rooms, without combat or grind.

Creator list

  • Priority A: Eeowna, Cozy K, Josh's Gaming Garden, The Cozy Gaming Club, Payton'sCorner, Miss Bubbles, and Liss the Lass. These are the folks where Common Room's tone should land naturally, and where a short demo segment could matter.
  • Priority B: smaller cozy streamers who regularly cover Steam demos, community-first multiplayer, or pixel art slice-of-life games. I have 34 names in the sheet, with notes on platform, usual posting cadence, and whether they prefer email or DM.
  • Watch list: variety creators who touched Palia, A Short Hike, Garden Story, Spiritfarer, or Sticky Business but do not identify as cozy-only. We will not blast them; only send if there is a specific angle.

Press and outlets

  • Indie/cozy: Wholesome Games newsletter, The Indie Informer, LadiesGamers, Checkpoint Gaming, Can I Play That, and Into Indie Games.
  • Broader games press: Rock Paper Shotgun, PC Gamer indie desk, Polygon, Eurogamer, Game Developer, and GamesRadar. For these, the angle is the multiplayer hangout design, not just “cozy game exists.”
  • Local/timezone-friendly: a small set of French and Japanese indie outlets so we are not making SF hours the center of gravity again.

Timing

  1. T-10 days: send warm creator emails with the trailer, Steam link, press kit, and a very short “why this might fit your channel” paragraph. No giant lore packet.
  2. T-7 days: press note goes out with embargo-free demo access, capsule description, screenshots, GIFs, and our preferred attribution.
  3. T-3 days: creator follow-up only for Priority A and only if we have something concrete to add, like demo build live or a cleaner trailer cut.
  4. Fest day 1: post a public creator-friendly thread with the demo link, key art, and three capture suggestions. Quietly answer DMs. No pile-on tagging.
  5. Fest days 3-5: share one round of coverage highlights from the studio account, prioritizing smaller creators first.

Owners

  • Antoine: final creator list, outbound email copy, press sheet hygiene, and all first sends.
  • Grace: press kit polish and screenshot/GIF selection, especially the cafe, library nook, game room, and conference hall loop.
  • Chloe: Steam page alignment, capsule copy, and making sure links do not send people to yesterday's draft universe.
  • Maya: public social posts during the Fest and deciding what we amplify from the studio account.
  • Hana: Japanese outlet pass and tone check on the translated capsule copy.

Materials needed

  • Press kit folder with logo, key art, 8 screenshots, 5 short GIFs, fact sheet, team boilerplate, and contact email.
  • One 30-second trailer cut and one 12-second vertical clip for creators who need fast context.
  • Steam demo link, build notes in plain English, and a known-issues note that does not sound like an apology spiral.

I will start sends once the kit is locked. If anything here looks risky, please nudge me in the sheet or in a separate thread so this one can stay as the quiet paper trail.

title: We're going for Steam Next Fest
content:
After last week's playtest, I think we have enough signal to make the call: we are aiming Common Room at a Steam Next Fest demo.

This is ambitious. It is also achievable. The shape of the game is finally visible to people who did not already have it in their heads, and that is because of the work everyone has put into the clubhouse, the rituals, the quiet multiplayer moments, the build pipeline, the Discord wrangling, the bugs nobody wanted to reproduce twice, and the thousand tiny art and sound decisions that made the place feel inhabited.

To be very clear: this is a stretch, not a death march. We will cut scope before we cut people. The demo needs to be honest, stable, and memorable. It does not need to contain every beautiful idea we have.

Maya will post the first planning thread today. For now, thank you. Take the win for a minute. Then let's make the version of Common Room strangers can fall in love with.

Already drafting the planning thread. The first version will cover:

  • demo promise
  • must-haves versus nice-to-haves
  • known production risks
  • date math without magical thinking

One unblocking question for Grace: can I name the target festival window in the planning thread, or should I keep it as "Next Fest demo" until Steam confirms the exact slot?

I'm in. Also putting a flag in the ground early: our demo should not try to explain the entire game. It should create one clean evening in the clubhouse where players understand, without a lecture, that the game is about being near each other on purpose.

My current instinct is cafe arrival, library nook discovery, one tiny group activity in the game room, and then the conference hall as the soft finale. If someone leaves saying "I get why I would invite friends here," we won. If they leave saying "there were many systems," we missed.

Strong opinion, loosely held, but I want us to protect that feeling aggressively.

yes. My engineering posture for this:

if (feature.risksDemoStability) cut(feature)
if (feature.requiresNewBackendShape) probably_cut(feature)
if (feature.makesPlayersInviteAFriend) fight_for(feature)

We can ship a crisp vertical slice. We cannot ship a museum of half-wired intentions.

For art, I want the demo to feel like one finished place, not four rooms competing for attention. We can simplify prop density, reuse lighting language, and keep the 16x16 tile readability strict.

I will be gentle about cuts and very annoying about palette discipline. Both are love.

Repro steps:

  1. Read Grace's post.
  2. Open last playtest notes.
  3. Observe team confidence increasing beyond expected bounds.

Expected: cautious optimism. Actual: cautious optimism with unusually good bug-triage implications.

discord people are going to lose it when we can say this out loud 😭 The playtesters kept using words like "gentle", "easy to stay in", and "I wanted my roommate to see this." That last one is the whole thing for me.

Yes. Also: what's the trailer moment?

I have three candidates already, all from the playtest: everyone gathering around the cafe counter, the first synchronized sit in the library nook, and the conference hall reveal when players realize it is not a menu screen but a place. I will bring receipts in the marketing thread.

Okay!!! I am trying very hard not to turn this comment into a full netcode diary, but the short version is: the last playtest gave us way cleaner movement data than I expected, and I think the "people naturally orbiting the cafe" behavior is real, not a fluke.

I have two fixes queued that should make room joins less crunchy. Will put them in the engineering thread after breakfast because apparently typing at 2am does not count as planning.

This might be an obvious question, but for the demo do we prefer a smaller number of interactions that are very polished, or more interactable objects so the room feels generous? I assume smaller/polished, but I want to calibrate before I pick up tasks.

Not obvious, and exactly the right question. My vote is fewer interactions with clearer social meaning. A chair that reliably helps two players hang out beats twelve props that each say "press E" and then shrug.
I can make the conference hall reveal read better with two animation passes: crowd idle timing and curtain movement. Small scope. High feeling.
i'll start shaping the demo mix around arrival, pause, and gathering. less music that tells you how to feel, more sound that makes the room feel like it was there before you logged in.

The demo can treat the clubhouse like a place with a first memory. Not exposition, just small proof that people have used these rooms before you arrived: a borrowed mug, a signed-up board, a book left open in the library nook. The player is not being onboarded into a product; they are being welcomed into a habit.

The CI is my garden and apparently we are inviting the neighborhood over. I will start pruning flaky checks today.

Serious version: demo branch rules, nightly build labels, and Steam packaging checklist incoming. If a build step requires folk magic, it does not get to be a build step anymore.

I love the energy in here. I am also going to be the person who says the calendar part out loud: stretch does not mean heroic sleep math.

Please put PTO and family constraints in the planning thread early. We can plan around reality much better than around everyone pretending to be infinitely available.

I have a few tiny thumbnail comps for the "strangers fall in love with it" version of the lobby/cafe path. They are very rough, please imagine better hands drew them.

Will post in art instead of turning Announcements into my sketchbook.

Maya: yes, name the target as Steam Next Fest in the planning thread. We do not need to promise a public date today.

And thank you, everyone. This is the tone I hoped for: high standards, clear cuts, no martyrdom.

Planning thread is up. I kept the first pass intentionally boring in the best way: dates, owners, risks, decision points.

Please put big feelings here and concrete commitments there so Future Us can find things without archaeology.

title: Demo build smoke test findings (live)
content:
Live smoke pass on Next Fest demo candidate 0.9.31. Demo opens while this thread is still warm, so I am logging as I go instead of pretending this is a tidy report. Maya has not read this yet, which is probably healthy.

Blockers

  1. Cafe join freeze. Repro: host in central lobby, second player joins, host enters cafe, second player opens emote wheel during room fade. Result: host lands in cafe, second client keeps music playing but input/render freeze. Repro 3/3 on Windows, 1/2 on Mac.
  2. Intel Mac black screen after language select. Repro: fresh install, launch, pick English, continue. Result: cursor visible, audio ok, screen black. Logs mention missing atlas page clubhouse_props_02. Repro 2/2 on Priya's old Mac mini.

Polish nits

  • Conference hall south chairs draw over player hair for one frame after sitting.
  • Library nook ladder tile still says Sit. I did sit. It was not dignified.
  • Game room board prompt says Join round even when the table is full.

Delightful surprise

Three external testers made an actual cafe line without being asked. The counter collision plus idle spacing reads as social behavior. Please do not fix this by accident.

I am on the cafe join freeze! I can repro locally with two clients if I spam emote wheel during RoomTransition.enter(). Looks like the input layer is getting re-enabled before the scene actor registry finishes swapping, which is a sentence I hate but can fix.

I can take the Intel Mac atlas issue. The build manifest has clubhouse_props_02.webp but the runtime loader asks for clubhouse_props_02.png on the non-metal fallback path. I found the branch and am making the extension lookup use the packed manifest instead of guessing.

if (transition.isActive) return InputBlockReason.RoomTransition;

Marcus, I would ship the boring guard first and untangle the registry timing after the demo is not actively breathing on us.

Cafe freeze fix is in the demo hotfix branch and the repro now fails to fail, which is my favorite category of result! Leaving a tiny follow-up ticket for the registry weirdness, but the blocker is patched.
Intel Mac fix pushed too. I tested fresh install twice on the mini, language select now lands in the lobby, and the cafe line survived. I did not touch collision. I know better now.

title: Capsule art, near-final, need eyes before it goes on the page
content:
Near-final pass on direction B for the Steam capsule. This is the version I want to put on the page unless someone catches a real problem in the next few hours.

What I am looking for: crop issues, logo readability, whether the crew reads as “shared clubhouse” at capsule size. What I am not looking for: a new lighting direction, a new logo placement philosophy, or my fifth pass at making everyone’s heads visible without turning the lobby into a class photo.

I have already checked the small capsule once and it holds up better than I expected, but fresh eyes before this becomes public would help.

Tiny crop note from the person who once lost a whole character behind a filename badge: at small capsule size, the bottom row of shoes is doing less than the top-left logo breathing room. I would crop up by maybe 8-10 px and give the logo a little more air. The faces still read, and the lobby feels warmer because we get less floor.

For store readability, I think direction B is the right one. My only worry is that the logo gets polite when it needs to be a little rude. Not bigger by much, maybe 4%, but enough that the hook survives beside louder games.

The trailer moment here is “everyone has a place to stand,” which is exactly what we need. Please keep the warm lobby chaos. That part is working.

I am trying Tomas’s crop and Antoine’s very small logo nudge now. If either makes the composition start shouting, I am backing it out. The current read is calm-on-purpose and I want to keep that.

Will post the export back here before it goes to Steam, assuming the demo page does not need it in the next ten minutes.
